Germany’s Job Seeker Visa: My Personal Experience and Everything You Need to Know # I researched it on YouTube, applied, received a response a year later. Here’s exactly what happened — and what you should do today.
One of the most common questions from people in Turkey (and across the world) who want to move to Germany is: “How can I go there to find work if I don’t already have a job?”
The answer: Germany’s Job Seeker Visa (Jobsucher-Visum), issued as a national visa for the purpose of job searching.
This isn’t theory. I went through this process myself.
🇩🇪 What This Visa Actually Does # Germany allows foreign nationals living abroad to enter the country specifically to search for work — for up to six months. You don’t need a job offer to apply. You apply, receive the visa, travel to Germany, and conduct your job search in person.
If you find employment during this period, you transition to a work permit. If not, you return home.
It’s a genuine pathway — not a loophole — designed to attract international talent.
📋 How to Apply # Applications are made through VFS Global, which handles German visa applications in Turkey (and many other countries). The relevant site: https://www.vfsglobal.com/germany/turkey
The process:
Visit the VFS Global website — navigate to the national visa section Select job search as your purpose Prepare the required documents and book an appointment Pay the application fee — it’s modest, not significant Wait My experience: I applied, and approximately one year later I received an email from the German embassy. The message was essentially: “Your application has been processed. If you wish to continue, please click the link below.”
At that point, I had obtained Bulgarian citizenship and the EU pathway was open to me. I didn’t continue. But the system worked — a response came.
⚡ The Most Important Advice: Apply Today # The waiting period is long. This is the central practical point:
Apply on the day you read this.
You will have time to prepare while you wait. You don’t need everything ready before you apply — you just need to start the clock.
📝 What to Do While You Wait # 1. Start Applying to German Companies Immediately # Begin sending job applications to German companies as soon as you’ve submitted your visa application. Why?
Because if you’re asked during the visa process why you want this visa, the strongest answer is: “I have been applying to companies in Germany for the past year, but without being present in the country, I cannot progress through the hiring process.”
Apply to at least a few companies per week and keep a log of your applications. This documentation matters.
2. Build a Strong CV — in Two Languages # AI tools make this significantly easier than it used to be. Key points:
Prepare both an English and a German version German CV conventions differ from Turkish ones: photos are standard, personal details are more comprehensive, format expectations are specific A German-language CV signals serious intent and stands out considerably 3. Collect Reference Letters # This is a Germany-specific factor that many people underestimate: reference letters (Arbeitszeugnis or Referenzschreiben) carry significant weight in German hiring.
Contact previous employers in Turkey and request written references. English or German versions are most useful. These letters are one of the most effective ways to build credibility with a German employer who doesn’t know you.
4. Learn German # Many international companies in Germany will accept English-language applications and conduct interviews in English. But if you’re going to live in Germany, German is not optional — it’s essential for daily life, bureaucracy, healthcare, and building genuine social connections.
Use the waiting period to reach at least B1 level, ideally B2.
Where to learn:
My recommendation: university language programmes. In Turkey, the Türk-Alman Üniversitesi and language departments at several Istanbul universities offer structured German courses with qualified teachers. Most now have online options — delivered through platforms like Google Classroom — so you don’t need to attend in person. Materials are archived, classes are recorded, and you can study at your own pace.
🗺️ The Process at a Glance # 1. Apply via VFS Global today ↓ 2. Start applying to German companies regularly ↓ 3. Prepare CV in English + German ↓ 4. Collect reference letters from past employers ↓ 5. Learn German (target: B1/B2) ↓ 6. Receive embassy response (~6–12 months) ↓ 7. Complete the process and travel to Germany 💬 Final Thought # This process requires patience. But if you use the waiting time well — German language, polished CV, reference letters, consistent applications — you will arrive in Germany significantly better prepared than most people who make this move.
I stopped the process because circumstances changed. But I applied, I saw how the system works, and I received a response. It works.
Start today.
Part of the Working & Living in Europe series. For a detailed 20-topic comparison of life in Germany vs Turkey, see the Turkey vs Germany series.

Netherlands vs Luxembourg vs Germany: Why I Chose Germany # I researched all three seriously. Here’s the side-by-side comparison — and the honest reasons behind my final decision.
Before I moved to Munich, I spent a long time looking at three countries: the Netherlands, Luxembourg, and Germany. I had job interviews in all three. I received sponsorship offers. I ran the numbers.
This is the comparison I wish had existed when I started.
📊 The Numbers: What You Actually Take Home # Salary comparisons between countries are meaningless without accounting for tax, healthcare costs, and housing. Here’s a simplified but realistic picture:
Netherlands Luxembourg Germany Gross salary (example) €90,000 €70,000 €80,000 Effective tax rate ~40% (without 30% ruling) ~20% ~35% Healthcare cost (family) ~€500/month (individual) ~€200/month (social) ~€600/month (shared GKV) Typical family rent €1,750–2,500+ €2,250–3,000+ €1,500–2,200 (Munich) Estimated net after rent Variable Variable Comparable The conclusion: net take-home across the three countries is more similar than gross salaries suggest. The Netherlands’ 30% ruling helps; Luxembourg’s low taxes help. Germany’s higher taxes are partially offset by lower rents (outside Munich) and employer-matched insurance.
🗣️ Language: A Real Differentiator # Netherlands Luxembourg Germany Primary language needed Dutch (but English suffices) French (English works in international jobs) German (essential for daily life) English sufficiency Excellent Good in companies Limited outside work Language learning difficulty Moderate Moderate (French) High If language ease is your priority, the Netherlands wins. If you already speak German or French, Luxembourg or Germany become stronger options.
🏠 Housing: The Make-or-Break Factor # Netherlands Luxembourg Germany Availability Serious shortage Very limited Tight (Munich), better elsewhere Min. family rent ~€1,750 ~€2,250 ~€1,500 Mortgage access After 1 year Standard Standard (3–5 years typical) Cross-border living Not typical Very common (France/Belgium) Not typical Luxembourg’s cross-border commuter option is a genuine differentiator — but primarily for EU citizens. For Turkish citizens requiring residency in the country of employment, this option is more restricted.
🧒 For Families: The Comparison That Mattered Most to Me # Netherlands Luxembourg Germany Childcare cost Subsidised, income-based Free for working parents Subsidised (Kita) Primary school pressure Low Low Low Homework (early years) Minimal Minimal Minimal Child happiness outcomes World-leading Good Very good Healthcare for children Individual insurance Social system GKV covered All three countries take child development seriously. The Netherlands has the strongest research outcomes. Luxembourg has the most generous childcare policy. Germany has the most established and consistent system.
🏥 Healthcare Comparison # Netherlands Luxembourg Germany System type Individual purchase Social insurance Statutory (GKV) Family cost ~€500/month out of pocket Employer/employee shared Employer/employee shared Coverage breadth Basic (supplementary needed) Broad (~80% covered) Broad Verdict Most expensive for families Good Most comprehensive Germany’s GKV system, despite higher gross premiums, is employer-matched and covers the whole family. For families, this is a meaningful advantage over the Netherlands’ individual-purchase model.
🌍 International Community # Netherlands Luxembourg Germany % foreign residents ~15% ~50% ~15% (Munich higher) Turkish community Moderate Small Large and established Integration support Good Good Good Loneliness risk Moderate Higher (small country) Moderate For Turkish expats specifically, Germany — and Munich in particular — has the largest, most established Turkish community in Europe. This matters more than it might seem, especially in the early months.
⚖️ Final Comparison Table # Dimension Netherlands Luxembourg Germany Net salary (realistic) ⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐ Tax efficiency ⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐ Healthcare (family) ⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Housing (availability) ⭐⭐ ⭐ ⭐⭐⭐ Language ease ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐ Family & childcare ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Turkish community ⭐⭐ ⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Stability & systems ⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ City life quality ⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 💬 Why Germany Won — For Me # No spreadsheet makes this decision. But when I looked honestly at what mattered most to my family, Germany kept coming out ahead on the factors I weighted most heavily:
The Turkish community. Arriving in a new country with an established community that speaks your language, shares your food, and understands your references is not a small thing. Germany — and Munich specifically — has this in a way neither the Netherlands nor Luxembourg can match.
Healthcare for the family. Germany’s GKV system covers everyone. The Netherlands’ individual-purchase model would have cost our family significantly more every month.
Stability and rule of law. All three countries score well here, but Germany’s institutional depth — its legal frameworks, its civic culture, its long-term predictability — gave me the most confidence.
Munich as a city. The Alps. The beer gardens. The cycling infrastructure. The international job market. Munich is genuinely one of the best cities in Europe to build a life in, and that matters beyond the numbers.
Luxembourg would have been my second choice — the tax advantage and free public transport are real, and the international community is welcoming. The housing situation and the distance from Turkey (both practically and culturally) tipped the balance.
The Netherlands was close. The English-language advantage and the progressive education system were compelling. The healthcare cost structure and housing scarcity were not.
This is part of the Europe for Turkish Expats series. For my detailed 20-topic comparison of Germany and Turkey, see the Turkey vs Germany series.

Working & Living in Luxembourg: Europe’s Best Kept Secret? # Free public transport. Low taxes. A country where half the population are immigrants. Here’s the full picture.
Luxembourg doesn’t come up often in conversations about where to move in Europe. It should.
I had a job interview there. I researched the country seriously. And while I ultimately chose Germany, what I found in Luxembourg surprised me — both the advantages and the very real challenges.
🇱🇺 The Basics: A Country Unlike Any Other # Luxembourg is tiny — about 2,600 square kilometres, roughly the size of a large city. Yet it’s one of the wealthiest countries in the world by GDP per capita, home to major European institutions, and a hub for finance, technology, and logistics.
Here’s what makes it genuinely unusual: approximately 50% of the population are immigrants or foreign nationals. This isn’t a country where being a newcomer makes you stand out. It’s a country built on international mobility.
The immediate practical implication: there’s an established infrastructure for integration, multilingual services are the norm, and the social environment is more internationally oriented than almost anywhere else in Europe.
🗣️ Three Languages (Plus English) # Luxembourg officially operates in three languages: Luxembourgish, French, and German. In practice, French dominates in professional and official settings — unsurprising given that France and Belgium border the country to the south and west.
English is widely spoken in international companies, of which there are many. If you’re joining a multinational — in finance, tech, or logistics — you can operate professionally in English without issue.
The challenge comes in daily life and bureaucratic interactions, where French is the predominant language. Unlike the Netherlands (where English genuinely suffices for everything), Luxembourg requires some French for full integration.
The geography helps: with Belgium, France, Germany, and the Netherlands all within short driving distance, the multilingual environment feels natural rather than imposed.
💰 Taxes: The Real Advantage # This is where Luxembourg genuinely shines.
While countries like Germany and the Netherlands can see income tax rates climbing toward 40–45% for higher earners, Luxembourg’s effective rates are meaningfully lower — typically around 20% for many professional salaries.
In practice, this means: if a comparable role offers €80,000–90,000 gross in Germany or the Netherlands, but €70,000 in Luxembourg, the net take-home may be roughly equivalent — or better in Luxembourg.
The calculation matters. It’s not the gross number that determines your quality of life. It’s what lands in your account after tax.
🚌 Free Public Transport: A Genuine Game-Changer # In 2020, Luxembourg became the first country in the world to make all public transport permanently free — buses, trams, and trains across the entire national network.
For a family, this eliminates a significant recurring expense. For daily commuters, it removes friction entirely. For environmental and quality-of-life reasons, it’s a meaningful policy commitment.
The network is well-developed and connects Luxembourg City to surrounding regions efficiently. Given that many residents live outside the capital (often in neighbouring countries — more on that below), the system is designed for cross-border use.
🏥 Healthcare: Better Than the Netherlands # Unlike the Netherlands’ individual-purchase model, Luxembourg operates a social health insurance system (Caisse Nationale de Santé) that functions more like Germany’s — mandatory, employer-partially-funded, and comprehensive.
When you visit a doctor, the insurance covers approximately 80% of the cost. You pay the remaining 20%, or sometimes less. Prescriptions work similarly.
This is significantly more favourable than the Dutch system, where the basic individual premium alone runs €500+ per month for a family. In Luxembourg, the cost is shared, the coverage is broader, and the out-of-pocket exposure is lower.
🏠 Housing: The Biggest Problem # Here is where Luxembourg’s appeal runs into serious practical reality.
Housing is extremely expensive — more so than the Netherlands, and dramatically more than Germany. When I was researching, realistic rents for a family apartment in or near Luxembourg City started at €2,250 per month and climbed quickly from there.
The supply is limited. The demand from a large, internationally mobile, well-paid workforce is intense. Unlike in the Netherlands, there is no one-year mortgage shortcut to offset rental costs.
For many people — especially those with EU citizenship — the solution is to live in a neighbouring country and commute. France, Belgium, and Germany are all within 30–60 minutes of Luxembourg City by car. Tens of thousands of people do this daily. Rents in border towns in France or Belgium can be dramatically lower than inside Luxembourg itself.
For Turkish citizens, this option is more complicated — cross-border living arrangements interact with residency permit requirements in ways that require careful legal advice.
🧒 Childcare: Free for Working Families # For families with young children, Luxembourg has a notable advantage: childcare and early education are free for working parents.
Crèches (nurseries) and early schooling are provided without charge to families where both parents are employed. This is a substantial financial benefit that doesn’t appear in salary comparisons but makes a real difference to monthly budgets.
⚖️ Honest Assessment # Topic Luxembourg Tax rates Low (~20% effective for many) Public transport Free — entire country Healthcare Social system, ~80% covered Housing cost Very high (€2,250+ for families) Cross-border living Common, practical for EU citizens Childcare Free for working parents Languages needed French (dominant), English works in international roles International community ~50% of population — very welcoming Size & lifestyle Small, quiet, high quality of life 💬 My Conclusion # Luxembourg is a serious option that most people researching European relocation overlook. The tax advantage, free public transport, social healthcare, and genuinely international community are all real benefits.
The housing situation is the central challenge. Unless you’re willing to live in a neighbouring country and commute — which many people do successfully — accommodation costs will absorb a large portion of the salary advantage.
For EU citizens, the cross-border option makes Luxembourg very attractive. For non-EU citizens, the calculation is more complex.
I came away from my research impressed — and with genuine regret that it didn’t work out. For the right person with the right situation, Luxembourg may be the best answer in Europe.

Working & Living in the Netherlands: What I Learned Before Moving # I applied for jobs there, got a sponsorship offer, and researched everything. Here’s what nobody tells you.
The Netherlands was my first serious target when I decided to move abroad. I spent months researching, applied to companies, and got far enough in the process to receive a sponsorship offer. I didn’t end up moving there — but I learned an enormous amount along the way.
This is what I wish I’d found in one place when I started.
🔍 Where to Start: Finding the Right Resources # Most expat job searches start on LinkedIn or generic job boards. But for the Netherlands specifically, there’s a much better resource: Welcome to NL — a platform commissioned by the Dutch Ministry of Economic Affairs, built specifically to attract and guide international talent.
It covers everything: jobs by sector and region, immigration and work permits, housing, healthcare, childcare, and a step-by-step relocation app. If you’re seriously considering the Netherlands, this should be your first stop.
The sectors with the most international recruitment are high-tech, semiconductors, AI, agriculture & food, life sciences, and logistics. Companies like ASML in Eindhoven are world leaders in their fields and actively hire internationally.
💼 Sponsorship & Work Permits # As a Turkish citizen (at the time without EU citizenship), I needed a company willing to sponsor a work permit. This is called the kennismigrant (knowledge migrant) route in Dutch.
The good news: the Netherlands actively recruits international talent and has a structured legal pathway for it. Companies familiar with the process can sponsor efficiently. The company I spoke with had done it before and was straightforward about it.
The requirement: your salary must meet a minimum threshold (updated annually) to qualify for the knowledge migrant permit. For most professional roles, this isn’t a problem — Dutch tech and engineering salaries are competitive.
💰 The 30% Ruling: What It Is and What Changed # The 30% ruling is a Dutch tax incentive for highly skilled migrants recruited from abroad. The idea: if you’re relocating from more than 150km outside the Netherlands, you may qualify for a tax exemption on 30% of your gross salary for several years.
In practice, on a €100,000 gross salary, instead of being taxed on the full amount, you’re taxed on €70,000. This significantly increases your net take-home pay.
Important update: the rules changed. The original scheme offered 30% for five years. The updated version runs as follows: 30% for the first two years, 20% for years three and four, and 10% in the fifth year. Still valuable — but less generous than it was.
🏥 Healthcare: The Hidden Cost # This was one of the biggest surprises in my research. Unlike Germany’s employer-matched statutory insurance, the Netherlands requires individuals to purchase their own basic health insurance (zorgverzekering).
The cost: roughly €120–130 per person per month for basic coverage. For a family of four, that’s approximately €500 per month coming directly out of your net salary — on top of everything else.
The coverage at the basic level is also narrower than Germany’s GKV. Dental, physiotherapy, and certain specialist treatments often require supplementary insurance (aanvullende verzekering) at additional cost.
When you factor this into your net salary calculation, the numbers look meaningfully different than they appear on the contract.
🏠 Housing: The Hardest Part # The Netherlands has a well-documented housing crisis, particularly in Amsterdam, Utrecht, and Eindhoven. Demand dramatically outpaces supply, and rental prices reflect this.
When I was researching, minimum realistic rents in the Amsterdam area started around €1,750 per month for a basic apartment. For a family, you’re looking at significantly more.
There is one interesting counterbalance: the mortgage system. After just one year of employment in the Netherlands, you can apply for a mortgage (hypotheek). Some people I researched had found that a monthly mortgage payment on a property — around €900–1,000 — was actually lower than the equivalent rent for a similar space.
This is unusual in European terms. Most countries require several years of residency and income history before mortgage eligibility. The Dutch one-year rule is a genuine advantage for people planning to stay long-term.
🧒 Children & Education # Child development philosophy in the Netherlands is notably progressive. In the early primary years, homework is minimal or absent — the focus is on play, social development, and building confidence rather than academic performance.
The results speak for themselves: Dutch children consistently rank among the happiest in the world, and the Netherlands regularly appears in the top ten of global wellbeing indices.
For working families, there’s a practical system worth knowing: after school (which often ends around 2–3pm), many areas have BSO (buitenschoolse opvang — out-of-school care) centres. These collect children from school and keep them until parents finish work. It’s an additional cost, but it’s structured and widely used.
🌍 The Language Question # One of the Netherlands’ biggest advantages for international arrivals: English is spoken at a genuinely high level by the vast majority of the population. You can navigate daily life, work in many companies, and integrate socially without learning Dutch immediately.
This is a meaningful practical difference from Germany, where German is essential for most official and social contexts from day one.
Dutch is not impossible to learn — it has structural similarities to English and German. But it’s not a prerequisite for getting started.
⚖️ Honest Assessment # Topic Netherlands International job market Very active, structured English sufficiency Excellent 30% ruling Valuable, recently reduced Healthcare cost Individual expense (~€500/month family) Housing availability Serious shortage, high prices Mortgage access Fast (1 year) — unusual advantage Child education philosophy Progressive, low-pressure Work-life balance Among the best in Europe 💬 My Conclusion # The Netherlands is a genuinely attractive destination — progressive, international, English-friendly, with strong worker protections and a remarkable child development philosophy. The 30% ruling makes the financial case even stronger for high earners.
The housing situation is the biggest practical obstacle. Without a clear plan for accommodation before you arrive, the process becomes very stressful very quickly.
I came close to making this move. What ultimately pointed me toward Germany was a combination of factors — including the healthcare structure and the housing uncertainty. But for the right person in the right sector, the Netherlands remains one of Europe’s strongest options.

content:
Quality of Life & Happiness: The Final Verdict # After everything — is Germany better? The honest answer is more complicated than yes or no.
This is the closing article in the Turkey vs Germany series. If you’ve read the others, you’ll know I’ve tried to be honest about both sides throughout. This is where I pull it all together.
What “Quality of Life” Actually Means # Quality of life is not a single number. It’s the sum of a thousand daily experiences: how safe you feel walking to the shop, how the school run goes, whether you trust the system when something goes wrong, whether you can afford the life you want, whether you feel at home.
Different people weight these things differently. A single young professional weights them differently than a family with young children. Someone who values adventure weights them differently than someone who values stability.
I’m writing this as someone who moved with a family, is raising a child, and is building a long-term life. That shapes everything I’ve written.
What Germany Does Better # Predictability. The systems work. The rules apply to everyone. You can plan your life — financially, professionally, logistically — without constantly hedging against instability.
Safety. Physical safety, legal safety, financial safety. The infrastructure of a functioning state, consistently applied.
Children’s wellbeing. The school system, the Kita, the cycling culture, the parks, the long-term thinking about child development — Germany takes this seriously in ways that show up in outcomes.
Worker rights. The floor is high. Holiday, parental leave, protection against dismissal — these change daily life in ways that accumulate into genuine freedom.
Environmental quality. Clean air, green spaces, cycling infrastructure, recycling culture, proximity to mountains and forests. Munich specifically is one of the most livable cities in Europe for a reason.
What Turkey Does Better # Human warmth. The richness of social connection, the hospitality, the feeling of being genuinely welcomed and known. Germany’s civic trust is admirable; Turkey’s human warmth is irreplaceable.
Food. I will never stop missing Turkish food. The freshness, variety, culture, and price of eating well in Turkey is not replicated anywhere in Europe.
Responsiveness. When something breaks — practically, institutionally, medically — Turkey moves fast. The human dimension of service culture in Turkey is genuinely superior.
Family proximity. Not everyone has left their family behind. For those who haven’t, the multi-generational warmth of Turkish family life is something Germany’s model simply doesn’t offer.
Entrepreneurial energy. Turkey’s hustle culture, its improvisation, its ability to make things happen through relationships — there’s a vitality to it that Germany’s structured efficiency sometimes lacks.
The Honest Summary # Dimension Turkey Germany Rule of law ⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Economic stability ⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Education ⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Healthcare ⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Human warmth ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐ Food culture ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐ Work-life balance ⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Family & social life ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐ Children’s environment ⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Bureaucracy ⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐ Nature & environment ⭐⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ My Verdict # I moved for my children. I stayed because of what I found.
Germany gave my family something I couldn’t build in Turkey at this point in history: a foundation. Stable, fair, safe, and genuinely invested in the long-term wellbeing of the people who live here.
Turkey gave me — still gives me — roots. Language, memory, warmth, food, family, identity. Nothing in Germany replaces those things. I don’t ask it to.
The question was never “which country is better.” The question was “which country is better for us, now, given what we need.”
For us, the answer was Germany.
Your answer might be different. And that’s completely fine.
Thank you for reading this series. If any of these articles resonated — or if you strongly disagree — I’d genuinely love to hear from you in the comments.

content:
Climate & Seasons: Turkey vs Germany # I knew German winters would be grey. I didn’t know how grey. Or how beautiful the summers would be.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Sun as Default # Turkey’s climate varies dramatically by region — the Aegean and Mediterranean coasts offer warm, dry summers and mild winters; the Black Sea region is lush and rainy; central Anatolia gets genuine cold snaps in winter.
But the general experience of Turkish climate, especially in major western cities, is of sun as the default setting. Long summers, abundant light, warm evenings that invite outdoor life well into October. The relationship between weather and social life is intimate — people spill out onto streets, sit in tea gardens, stay outdoors late.
Winters in cities like Istanbul can be grey and damp, but they’re short. Spring arrives decisively. The seasonal rhythm is clear and generally kind.
🇩🇪 Germany (Bavaria): Four Real Seasons # Munich sits in southern Bavaria, close to the Alps — which gives it a climate that’s more varied and dramatic than the rest of Germany.
Winters are cold, often grey, and can feel very long. November through February brings limited daylight, frequent cloud cover, and temperatures that hover around freezing. Snow is possible but not always reliable in the city itself; the mountains nearby, however, get excellent snow.
Spring in Munich is genuinely beautiful — cherry blossoms, the English Garden coming alive, the city’s famous Frühlingsfest and eventually the outdoor beer garden season opening.
Summer is magnificent. Long days, warm temperatures, the Isar river for swimming, beer gardens full until 11pm, people on bikes everywhere. Munich summers are among the best urban summer experiences in Europe.
Autumn in Bavaria is Herbst — golden, crisp, Oktoberfest, pumpkins, fog rolling in from the Alps. It’s spectacular before it turns grey.
Seasonal Affective Disorder is a real phenomenon here. The grey months affect people. Vitamin D deficiency is common. Light therapy lamps are sold everywhere. Acknowledging this is not weakness — it’s climate literacy.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey (West) Munich / Bavaria Sunshine hours High Moderate Winter severity Mild to moderate Cold, grey Summer quality Hot and long Warm, spectacular Seasonal variation Moderate Strong four seasons Alpine access Limited 1 hour to skiing Spring beauty Good Excellent Winter sports Rare Exceptional nearby 💬 My Take # The grey of German winter was harder than I expected. Not the cold — the cold I could dress for. The absence of light, day after day, wore on me in ways I hadn’t anticipated.
But Munich summers converted me. The combination of long days, Alpine backdrop, the Isar, and the city’s outdoor culture makes summer here genuinely joyful in a way I hadn’t experienced before.
And being one hour from ski slopes — that was an unexpected bonus. My relationship with seasons has completely changed. I now have a relationship with winter in a way I never did in Turkey.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Growing Up Between Two Cultures: Turkey vs Germany # My child speaks three languages and belongs fully to neither country. I’m not sure that’s a problem.
The Gift Nobody Talks About Enough # Children who grow up between two cultures get something most people never have: the ability to see any society from the outside. They know, instinctively, that the way things are done where they live is not the only way things can be done.
That’s a form of intellectual freedom. It’s also a burden. And it’s worth being honest about both.
🇹🇷 The Turkish Side # Turkey is not an abstract country to my child — it’s grandparents, food smells, a different pace, a language that connects to a whole other world. Visits to Turkey are not tourism. They’re return.
Turkish identity carries warmth, collectivism, a sense of belonging to something ancient and layered. Food, music, language, humour — these carry culture in ways that no school can teach and no policy can manufacture.
The challenge: if the child grows up primarily in Germany, Turkish can fade. Without deliberate effort — Turkish spoken at home, Turkish books, Turkish media, regular visits — the language weakens. And with the language, something of the culture weakens too.
🇩🇪 The German Side # Germany is where my child goes to school, makes friends, learns to navigate institutions, and develops independence. German is becoming a first language in practice, if not in heritage.
German civic culture — the rule-following, the trust, the structured approach to freedom — shapes a person. Growing up in Munich means growing up in a city that functions, where you can cycle to school alone at age 7, where public space is genuinely shared.
The challenge: German social culture can be closed. Without Turkish warmth in the home, a child raised purely in German social norms might become more reserved than their character actually calls for.
⚖️ What Bilingual Bicultural Actually Means # Dimension Turkish Heritage German Context Language Turkish at home German at school Social style Warm, expressive Reserved, structured Values Family, loyalty, hospitality Independence, civic duty Identity Where I’m from Where I live Advantage Bilingualism, cultural depth Opportunity, stability Challenge Maintaining roots Avoiding rootlessness 💬 My Take # I used to worry that my child would feel they don’t fully belong anywhere. I’ve come to think that’s the wrong frame.
Belonging fully to two places is possible. It requires effort — speaking Turkish at home without apology, visiting Turkey regularly, keeping the stories alive. And it requires accepting that your child will sometimes feel the tension of being between worlds.
That tension is not a failure. It’s the texture of a life lived at the intersection of cultures. Most of the most interesting people I know grew up somewhere like this.
What I want for my child is not to choose between Turkish and German — but to carry both, and to be richer for it.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Primary School: Turkey vs Germany # No homework until age 10. I had to read that twice.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Academic Pressure, Early # Turkish primary education begins at age 6 and places significant emphasis on academic performance from early on. Homework, exams, and ranking become part of a child’s life quickly. The pressure to perform well — to secure a place in a good secondary school, then university — begins earlier than many parents would choose.
Private tutoring (dershane culture) is widespread. Families who can afford it supplement school with private lessons, giving their children advantages in the competitive exam system.
The warmth of Turkish school culture is real — teachers often develop genuine relationships with students, and class communities can be tight-knit. But the system’s orientation toward exam performance over holistic development is a genuine limitation.
🇩🇪 Germany: Childhood First # German primary school (Grundschule) runs from age 6 to 10. The philosophy is fundamentally different from Turkey’s: childhood is protected. In the early years, there is little to no homework. Play, creativity, and social development are taken as seriously as academic skills.
Germany also has a structured streaming system: after Grundschule, children are recommended for one of three secondary school tracks:
Gymnasium — academic track, leads to Abitur (university entry) Realschule — intermediate track Hauptschule — vocational track This decision at age 10 carries significant weight — though pathways between tracks do exist, and the system is more flexible than it once was.
The Netherlands model (which influenced some German thinking) goes further: research there consistently shows that children who experience low-pressure early schooling grow into measurably happier adults. Germany is moving in this direction, though not uniformly.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany School start age 6 6 Early homework Yes Minimal Academic pressure High from early on Low in primary years Exam culture Intense Moderate School streaming Limited Structured at age 10 Private tutoring Widespread Less common Holistic development Secondary Central Teacher-student warmth High Moderate 💬 My Take # The no-homework policy in early years was one of the things that most influenced our decision to raise our child here. Not because I think academic achievement doesn’t matter — but because I believe childhood is short, and pressure applied too early does more harm than good.
Watching my child come home from school energized rather than exhausted is not a small thing. It shapes who they will become.
The streaming decision at age 10 makes me nervous — that’s young for a path to be set. But the German system does allow corrections, and the vocational track is genuinely respected here in a way it isn’t in Turkey.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Childcare & the Kita System: Turkey vs Germany # Finding a Kita spot in Munich is harder than finding a flat. But the system, when you’re in it, is remarkable.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Family First, State Second # In Turkey, childcare is predominantly family-based. Grandparents — especially grandmothers — play a central role in raising young children. This is cultural expectation as much as practical arrangement.
State-run nurseries and kindergartens exist, but availability and quality vary significantly. Private nurseries are an option for those who can afford them, but costs are substantial relative to average incomes.
The family model works well when the family is available and willing. It creates strong intergenerational bonds and continuity of care. But it places significant pressure on grandparents, and it ties parents — particularly mothers — to proximity to extended family.
🇩🇪 Germany: The Kita System # Kita (short for Kindertagesstätte) is Germany’s daycare and early education system for children from birth to school age. It combines childcare with structured early development — play-based learning, social skills, language development.
In theory, every child in Germany has a legal right to a Kita place from age one. In practice, demand far exceeds supply — especially in Munich. Applying for a Kita place begins during pregnancy in many cases. Waiting lists are long. Competition is real.
Once you have a place, the experience is generally excellent:
Qualified, trained Erzieherinnen (early childhood educators) Strong focus on outdoor play, independence, and social development Flexible hours that accommodate working parents Costs are income-based and often subsidized (Kitabeitrag) Kita also serves an integration function for expat families — children learn German, make local friends, and connect to the community in a way that parents sometimes struggle to.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany Primary care model Family-based Institutional (Kita) Availability Variable Legally guaranteed, practically scarce Quality consistency Uneven Generally high Cost Variable (private) Subsidized, income-based Working parent support Limited Strong (enables both parents to work) Intergenerational bonding Strong Less central Language development Turkish German (integration benefit) 💬 My Take # We applied for a Kita place before our child was born. That felt absurd at the time. It turned out to be necessary.
When we finally got a place, the quality exceeded my expectations. The educators were attentive, the environment was stimulating, and watching my child develop independence and language skills in that setting was genuinely moving.
What I miss from the Turkish model is the grandmother. The continuity of family care, the stories, the warmth of someone who loves your child unconditionally — no institution replicates that.
The Kita gives structure and quality. The family gives love and roots. The ideal, probably, is both.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Work Life & Work-Life Balance: Turkey vs Germany # In Turkey, you work hard and stay late. In Germany, you leave on time — and nobody apologises for it.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Hustle Culture with Heart # Turkish work culture is intense. Long hours are common and often expected. Being seen to work hard — staying late, being available, putting in visible effort — carries cultural weight. The boundary between work and personal life is porous.
The upside: Turkish workplaces are often warm and social. Colleagues become friends. There’s a human texture to professional relationships — shared meals, personal conversations, genuine interest in each other’s lives outside the office.
Entrepreneurship and hustle are deeply respected. There’s an energy in Turkish professional culture — a sense that things can always be done, deals can always be made, and relationships are the currency that makes it all work.
The downsides: burnout is common. Formal worker protections exist but are not always enforced. Job security can be precarious, especially in smaller companies.
🇩🇪 Germany: Structured, Protected, and Separated # German work culture operates on different principles. Hours are logged. Overtime is either compensated or avoided. Leaving work at 5pm is not laziness — it’s normal. Managers don’t expect you to answer emails at 10pm, and doing so doesn’t earn you extra credit.
The legal framework for workers is strong: minimum wage, mandatory holiday entitlement (typically 24–30 days), parental leave (Elternzeit) for both parents, strong protection against unfair dismissal. These aren’t perks — they’re baseline rights.
Work and private life are kept deliberately separate. Your colleagues may not know much about your personal life, and they won’t necessarily ask. The warmth is lower; the professionalism is higher.
Urlaubsreif — a wonderful German concept meaning “ready for holiday, exhausted and needing a break” — says something about how Germans think about rest: it’s earned, it’s necessary, and it’s taken seriously.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany Working hours Long Structured, protected Overtime culture Common Compensated or avoided Worker protections Moderate Strong Holiday entitlement 14 days (legal min.) 24–30 days typical Parental leave Limited Generous (Elternzeit) Workplace warmth High Lower, more formal Work-life separation Weak Strong Entrepreneurship culture Strong More risk-averse 💬 My Take # The first time I left work at 5pm without guilt, I didn’t know what to do with myself. It took months to stop feeling like I was doing something wrong.
German work culture gave me something I hadn’t known I was missing: the permission to stop. To have evenings. To take all my holiday. To be a parent without apologizing for it.
The warmth of Turkish professional relationships is something I miss. But the structure, the rights, and the genuine respect for non-work time have changed how I think about what a healthy working life looks like.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Cars & Traffic Culture: Turkey vs Germany # The Autobahn has no speed limit. Turkish drivers have no limits either. But the difference is everything.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Expressive, Fast, and Unpredictable # Driving in Turkey is an experience. Traffic rules exist but are interpreted creatively. Lane discipline is loose. Horns are communication tools. Pedestrian crossings are suggestions.
In Istanbul especially, traffic is a daily negotiation — a fluid, improvised dance between cars, motorcycles, buses, and pedestrians. It’s stressful for newcomers but has its own internal logic that locals navigate instinctively.
The deeper problem is accountability. When accidents happen — and they happen frequently — the consequences for negligent drivers can be minimal. Safety standards in construction, road maintenance, and vehicle inspection are inconsistently enforced. The feeling that something could go wrong and no one would be truly held responsible is not paranoia. It’s a reasonable reading of the system.
🇩🇪 Germany: Disciplined, Efficient, and Rule-Bound # German driving culture is almost the opposite. Lane discipline is strict. Merging follows rules. Traffic flows with a kind of collective coordination that feels almost choreographed.
The Autobahn — Germany’s famous motorway network — has sections with no speed limit (freie Fahrt). This sounds chaotic. In practice, it works because everyone follows the rules: slower traffic keeps right, faster traffic uses the left lane only for overtaking, and drivers signal consistently.
The TÜV (vehicle inspection) is mandatory and rigorous. Cars on German roads are maintained. Road surfaces are generally excellent. Pedestrian crossings are respected — drivers stop, often before you’ve even fully stepped off the pavement.
Parking rules are enforced strictly — often by fellow citizens reporting violations (as mentioned elsewhere in this series).
Public transport in Munich is exceptional. The U-Bahn, S-Bahn, tram, and bus network covers the city comprehensively, runs on time, and makes owning a car genuinely optional for many people.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany Traffic discipline Low High Speed culture Fast and unpredictable Fast but structured Pedestrian safety Poor Good Vehicle inspection Inconsistent Rigorous (TÜV) Road quality Variable Generally excellent Accident accountability Weak Strong Public transport Good in cities Excellent (Munich) Driving licence difficulty Moderate High (expensive, thorough) 💬 My Take # I felt physically safer on the road in Germany from the first week. Not because nothing ever goes wrong here — but because when something does, there are systems in place, inspections that happened, rules that were followed.
Getting a German driving licence is expensive and time-consuming if you need to start from scratch — but the process itself tells you something about how seriously road safety is taken.
And Munich’s public transport is so good that I genuinely use the car less than I ever did in Turkey. That’s a quality of life improvement I didn’t expect.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Elderly Care & Family Structure: Turkey vs Germany # In Turkey, family takes care of family. In Germany, the state steps in. Neither model is perfect.
🇹🇷 Turkey: The Family as Safety Net # In Turkish culture, caring for elderly parents is not a policy decision — it’s a moral expectation. When parents age, children take responsibility. Grandparents often move in with their children’s families, or live very close by. The extended family is the primary support structure.
This system has real strengths: elderly people are surrounded by love, connection, and familiar faces. They don’t face old age alone. The warmth of multi-generational living is something Turkey does genuinely well.
The challenges: this system places enormous pressure on families — especially women, who disproportionately carry the caregiving burden. There is less professional support infrastructure, and as Turkey urbanizes, the model strains as adult children move to different cities or countries.
🇩🇪 Germany: The State as Safety Net # Germany has built an extensive public infrastructure for elderly care. Pflegeversicherung (long-term care insurance) is mandatory and covers a significant portion of care costs. Professional care homes (Pflegeheime), home care services (Pflegedienste), and day care centers exist at scale.
The system means elderly people can receive high-quality professional care without relying entirely on family. It also means adult children are not legally obligated to upend their lives — though many still choose involvement.
The tradeoff: professional care, however excellent, is not family. Many elderly people in German care homes experience loneliness and social isolation in ways that are less common in Turkey’s family-based model.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany Primary care provider Family State / professional Emotional warmth High Depends on facility Caregiver burden High (on family) Distributed Professional infrastructure Limited Extensive Cost Low (informal) Covered by insurance Social isolation risk Low Real concern Legal family obligation Cultural (strong) Limited 💬 My Take # This topic is personal for anyone who has moved abroad and left aging parents behind. I feel the weight of that distance.
Turkey’s model carries more love. Germany’s model carries more structure. What both systems struggle with — in different ways — is the fundamental human need for connection in old age.
What I’ve concluded: the German system makes it possible to care for the elderly well. Whether that possibility is realized depends on the family, the facility, and the community around each person.
And for those of us living abroad: the guilt of distance doesn’t have a policy solution. It just has to be managed, visit by visit.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Being Turkish & Muslim in Germany: What It’s Actually Like # The headlines don’t tell the full story. Here’s my day-to-day experience.
🇹🇷 The Baseline # In Turkey, your cultural and religious identity is the default. You don’t have to explain Ramadan, justify your food choices, or navigate whether your name will affect how you’re perceived. You exist in a society that was built around the same culture and faith you grew up in.
That invisible comfort disappears when you move.
🇩🇪 The Reality in Germany # Germany has a large Turkish community — over three million people, making it the largest Turkish diaspora in the world. In Munich, Turkish bakeries, döner shops, mosques, and community organizations are well-established. You are not alone, and you are not invisible.
What works well:
Turkish community infrastructure is strong — grocery stores, halal butchers, cultural associations, mosques Younger generations of Germans are generally open and curious about other cultures In international work environments, diversity is genuinely valued Legal protections against religious discrimination are real and enforced What takes adjustment:
Pork is in everything — bread, sauces, packaged foods. Reading labels becomes automatic Alcohol is central to German social culture. Beer gardens, office parties, after-work drinks — navigating this as a non-drinker requires confidence Ramadan is not a public institution. No changed working hours, no collective atmosphere, no cannon shot at iftar Some interactions carry an undercurrent of assumptions — not always hostile, but present The name question: Turkish names are immediately recognizable. Research in Germany, as elsewhere in Europe, shows that names can affect responses to job applications and housing requests. This is a real phenomenon, not paranoia.
⚖️ Honest Assessment # Germany is not a uniformly welcoming place for everyone — no country is. There are regions, generations, and contexts where you will feel more or less at home.
But Germany also has genuine legal protections, a large and established Turkish community, and a civic culture that — at its best — values integration without requiring erasure of identity.
The key distinction I’ve found: Germany asks you to follow the rules, not to become German. As long as you respect the laws and the civic norms, there is real space for you to be who you are.
💬 My Take # Some days I feel completely at home here. Other days, I feel the distance — not from hostility, but from difference. That’s honest.
What I’ve found: being visibly yourself, being curious about German culture, and making genuine effort with the language opens more doors than anything else. People respond to effort. And Munich, as a cosmopolitan city, has more openness than you might expect.
The Turkish community here also provides something irreplaceable: a sense of continuity. People who understand your references, share your food, speak your language. That matters more than I anticipated.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
The Language Barrier: Turkey vs Germany # You can survive in Munich with English. But surviving and living are different things.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Your Language, Your World # Living in Turkey as a Turkish speaker means the world is fully open to you. Every bureaucratic process, every social interaction, every joke, every piece of paperwork — you navigate it all with complete fluency.
The psychological comfort of this is enormous and easy to take for granted. Until you don’t have it.
🇩🇪 Germany: The Daily Marathon # German is a complex language — long compound words, three grammatical genders, four cases, formal and informal registers. It takes years to become truly fluent, and even intermediate learners regularly hit walls.
Munich presents a particular challenge: beyond standard Hochdeutsch, many locals speak Bayerisch (Bavarian dialect), which can be nearly incomprehensible even to Germans from other regions. Ordering food at a traditional Wirtshaus and understanding the response is its own achievement.
What you can do with English alone:
Navigate most tourist and expat situations Work in international companies (Munich has many) Manage basic daily life in central areas What requires German:
Government offices (Ämter) — most staff speak only German Medical appointments (especially with older GPs) Dealing with landlords and housing contracts Reading official letters (Bescheide) — and Germany sends a lot of these Building real friendships with local Germans The bureaucracy especially does not accommodate language gaps. Letters arrive in formal German, with legal terminology, requiring action within strict deadlines. Missing a letter because you couldn’t understand it is not accepted as an excuse.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany Native language comfort Full Requires years of learning English sufficiency Limited outside tourism Works in international contexts Bureaucracy language Turkish German only (mostly) Dialect challenge Regional but manageable Bayerisch is genuinely hard Language learning support N/A Good (VHS, Goethe Institut) Integration without language Not applicable Possible but limited 💬 My Take # The language barrier was harder than I expected — not because German is impossible, but because the emotional cost of not fully expressing yourself is cumulative. You become a simpler version of yourself in a foreign language. Jokes don’t land. Nuance disappears. You feel less intelligent than you are.
My advice: start German before you arrive. Take it seriously. Even B1 level opens enormous doors — especially with authorities and in social settings.
And be patient with yourself. Fluency takes time, but progress is visible and genuinely rewarding.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Social Life & Making Friends: Turkey vs Germany # “Germans are cold.” Is it true? It’s more complicated than that.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Warm, Fast, and Deep # Turkish social culture is famously warm. Strangers become friends quickly. Hospitality is almost a competitive sport — guests must be fed, tea must be offered, no one leaves without feeling welcomed.
Friendships in Turkey tend to form fast and run deep. Social circles overlap: family, neighbours, school friends, work colleagues — they all mix. The boundaries between acquaintance and close friend collapse quickly.
The downside is that privacy can be harder to protect. Everyone has an opinion about your life choices. The collective nature of Turkish social culture is a warmth and a pressure simultaneously.
🇩🇪 Germany: Slow to Open, Loyal When They Do # Germans have a reputation for being cold or unfriendly. In my experience, this is a misreading. They’re not cold — they’re reserved. There’s a difference.
Germans tend to keep their private and public spheres very separate. Small talk with strangers is less common. Someone won’t smile at you on the street for no reason — not because they dislike you, but because that would feel performative to them.
But when a German decides you are their friend, that friendship is serious. They show up. They’re reliable. They don’t cancel plans. They remember what matters to you.
The challenge for expats is getting past the initial reserve. It takes longer. Joining clubs (Vereine), sports teams, language groups, or neighbourhood events are the most reliable ways in. Germans organize their social lives around structured activities more than spontaneous gatherings.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany Initial warmth Very high Low to moderate Speed of friendship Fast Slow Depth of friendship Deep Deep (once established) Hospitality culture Very strong Modest Privacy norms Low High Small talk culture Natural Less common Expat community Growing Large and active 💬 My Take # The loneliness of the first months in Germany is real — and nobody warns you about it enough. You move from a culture where social connection is effortless to one where you have to work for it. That’s hard.
But I’ve come to deeply respect German friendship. When someone here makes time for you, it means something. It wasn’t given out of politeness or habit — it was chosen.
The expat community in Munich also helped enormously. Finding other people navigating the same adjustment made everything easier.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Housing & Renting: Turkey vs Germany # Finding a flat in Munich is one of the hardest things I’ve done. Here’s the full picture.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Flexible but Volatile # Renting in Turkey is relatively straightforward in process — you find a place, negotiate with the landlord, sign a contract, move in. Personal relationships matter: landlords often prefer tenants they know or who come recommended.
Prices have risen dramatically in recent years, especially in Istanbul and other major cities, driven partly by inflation and partly by demand. The Turkish lira’s instability means rents are sometimes quoted in euros or dollars — a sign of how uncertain the market has become.
Lease terms tend to be more flexible. Month-to-month arrangements are common. Landlords and tenants often work things out informally. This can be convenient — or precarious, depending on which side you’re on.
🇩🇪 Germany: Competitive, Regulated, and Exhausting # Germany has a strong tenant protection culture. Rent control laws (Mietpreisbremse) exist in many cities. Landlords cannot simply evict tenants without serious cause. Long-term tenants have significant legal protections.
The problem: finding a flat in Munich is extremely competitive. Munich is one of the most expensive rental markets in Germany — and in Europe. Demand far exceeds supply. For a single apartment listing, landlords may receive 50–100 applications.
The German rental system involves important concepts:
Kaltmiete (cold rent): the base rent, excluding utilities Warmmiete (warm rent): includes heating and sometimes water Nebenkosten (additional costs): utilities, building maintenance, sometimes internet Kaution (deposit): typically three months’ cold rent, paid upfront At year end, you receive a Nebenkostenabrechnung — a detailed utility bill reconciliation showing exactly what you used vs. what you pre-paid. You may owe more, or get money back.
The process of applying for a flat requires a full documentation package: proof of income (Einkommensnachweise), credit check (Schufa), ID, sometimes a reference from a previous landlord. The system is formal, thorough, and slow.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany Finding a flat Moderate difficulty Very competitive (Munich) Tenant protections Moderate Very strong Rent transparency Variable Highly structured Utility billing Included or separate Detailed annual reconciliation Deposit 1–2 months typical 3 months cold rent Lease flexibility High More rigid Price stability Volatile More predictable 💬 My Take # The search for housing in Munich was genuinely stressful. Preparing full documentation packages, competing with dozens of other applicants, waiting weeks for responses — it tests your patience.
But once you’re in, the protections are real. Your landlord cannot easily remove you. The heating bill reconciliation at year end is fair — you pay for what you use. The system treats you like an adult with rights.
In Turkey, the process was easier to start. But the security at the end was less guaranteed.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Food & Eating Out: Turkey vs Germany # Turkish cuisine is world-famous for a reason. But German food culture has surprises of its own.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Food as Love Language # In Turkey, food is central to everything — family, hospitality, identity, celebration. Turkish cuisine is genuinely one of the richest in the world: fresh ingredients, regional variety, generations of technique passed down through families.
Eating out in Turkey is affordable and social. A full meal at a local lokanta (traditional eatery) costs very little. Breakfast culture is an event — kahvaltı spreads with dozens of small dishes, cheese, olives, eggs, tomatoes, tea. Street food is everywhere and often excellent: simit, börek, döner, midye dolma.
The social dimension of food in Turkey is hard to overstate. Meals are long, loud, and shared. You don’t eat alone if you can help it.
🇩🇪 Germany: Underrated, Honest, and Surprisingly Good # German food has an unfair reputation. Yes, it’s hearty — Schnitzel, Bratwurst, Bretzel, Schweinshaxe — but it’s honest food done well. Bavarian cuisine in Munich especially has a strong identity: Weißwurst for breakfast (before noon, traditionally), Obatzda cheese spread, dark bread, pretzels, beer gardens.
The beer garden (Biergarten) culture is something genuinely special. On a warm evening, sitting outside with a Maß (litre of beer), good food, families and strangers at shared tables — it’s one of Munich’s great pleasures.
Eating out is significantly more expensive than Turkey. A mid-range restaurant meal for two can easily cost €40–60. Tipping culture exists but is modest — rounding up or leaving 10% is standard.
The international food scene in Munich is excellent — Turkish, Italian, Japanese, Vietnamese, Lebanese. Germany’s immigrant communities have brought their cuisines with them, and the quality is often outstanding.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany Cuisine variety Exceptional Good (local + international) Eating out cost Affordable Expensive Breakfast culture Rich, elaborate Simple (bread, cheese, cold cuts) Street food Excellent Limited Social dining culture Very strong More reserved Beer garden culture None Iconic in Bavaria Fresh produce quality Excellent Good 💬 My Take # I miss Turkish food every day. The freshness, the variety, the price, the ritual of a long breakfast — nothing here fully replaces it. Munich has good Turkish restaurants, but it’s not the same as your mother’s kitchen or a neighbourhood lokanta.
What surprised me: I genuinely enjoy Bavarian food. It’s not fancy, but it’s real. And the beer garden — especially in summer — has become one of my favourite things about living here.
Food is where I feel the distance from home most acutely. But it’s also where I’ve discovered things I wouldn’t trade.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Shopping & Daily Errands: Turkey vs Germany # Sunday everything is closed. Yes, really. Here’s how to adapt — and what you might actually prefer.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Open, Loud, and Always Available # Shopping in Turkey is a full sensory experience. Markets are open late, corner shops (bakkal) are open almost around the clock, and if you run out of something at 11pm, there’s almost always somewhere to go.
Bazaars, weekly street markets (pazar), and fresh produce shops create a rhythm to the week that feels alive. Prices are negotiable in many contexts. Personal relationships with shopkeepers matter — your local bakkal might extend you credit, remember your usual order, and ask about your family.
Big shopping malls have also exploded across Turkish cities — open seven days a week, late hours, full of food courts and entertainment. Convenience is built into the culture.
The downside: price consistency is hard to rely on, especially as inflation has made costs unpredictable. And the sheer volume of choice and noise can be overwhelming.
🇩🇪 Germany: Efficient, Predictable, Closed on Sundays # Germany operates on a different rhythm entirely. Shops close early — often by 8pm on weekdays, sometimes earlier. And on Sundays, almost everything is closed. Supermarkets, clothing stores, furniture shops — closed.
This is protected by law (Ladenschlussgesetz). It exists to protect workers’ rest time, and most Germans support it. But for someone coming from Turkey, it takes serious adjustment.
What Germany does well: price transparency and quality consistency. Discounters like Aldi and Lidl offer genuinely good quality at low prices. Rewe, Edeka, and Kaufland cover the mid-range. There’s no haggling, no surprises — the price on the shelf is the price you pay.
Weekly markets (Wochenmarkt) exist here too — and they’re excellent. Fresh produce, local farmers, seasonal rhythms. In Munich especially, the Viktualienmarkt is a landmark worth visiting every week.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany Opening hours Long, flexible Early close, Sunday shut Sunday shopping Fully open Almost nothing open Price negotiation Common in markets Not a thing Price consistency Unpredictable Reliable Fresh produce access Excellent Good (markets + shops) Corner shop culture Very strong Limited Online shopping Good Excellent (Amazon, Otto) 💬 My Take # Sunday closures were genuinely hard at first. You learn to plan ahead — Friday shopping, stocked fridge, Sunday as an actual rest day. And after a while, you start to appreciate it. The city goes quiet. Families are out walking. There’s a pace to it.
But I still miss the bakkal. That relationship-based, always-open corner shop culture is something Germany simply doesn’t have — and probably never will.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Health System: Turkey vs Germany # From walking into any hospital to waiting weeks for a specialist — here’s what actually changed when I moved
Healthcare is one of those things you don’t think about until you need it. Moving countries means rebuilding your entire relationship with the medical system from scratch — new doctors, new insurance, new language, new rules.
Here’s what I found.
🇹🇷 Turkey: Fast, Accessible, But Uneven # Turkey’s healthcare system has improved enormously over the past two decades. The Sağlık Bakanlığı (Ministry of Health) rolled out a universal health insurance system (SGK) that covers most citizens, and new hospitals were built across the country.
The biggest advantage: speed and accessibility. In Turkey, you can walk into a hospital, see a specialist the same day, and get results fast. Customer service culture extends to healthcare — staff are responsive, systems are flexible, and if something goes wrong, someone picks up the phone.
The challenges: quality varies significantly between public and private hospitals. Trust in institutions — including medical ones — can be inconsistent. And the pressure to use private hospitals for better care means many people end up paying out of pocket despite having insurance.
🇩🇪 Germany: Thorough, Structured, But Slow # Germany has one of the most comprehensive healthcare systems in the world. Every employed person is automatically enrolled in statutory health insurance (gesetzliche Krankenversicherung or GKV). Your employer pays roughly half the premium. You are covered — fully — from day one.
The system is built around the Hausarzt (family doctor / GP). This is your first point of contact for almost everything. You register with one GP, they get to know you over time, and they refer you to specialists when needed.
The quality of care, when you get it, is excellent. Doctors are thorough. Prescriptions are careful. The system doesn’t over-medicate or rush.
The frustration: waiting times. Getting an appointment with a specialist can take weeks — sometimes months. If your Hausarzt refers you, it’s faster. But walking in as a new patient without a referral? Be prepared to wait.
Sunday and public holidays mean almost nothing is open. Emergency care exists, but for non-urgent issues, you wait.
⚖️ The Real Comparison # Turkey Germany Access speed Fast Slow for specialists Insurance coverage SGK (universal) GKV (universal) Quality consistency Variable Generally high Emergency responsiveness Excellent Good GP system Weak (often skipped) Strong (Hausarzt central) Out-of-pocket costs Often high (private) Low with GKV Language barrier None Can be significant 💬 My Take # The German system gives me peace of mind in a way the Turkish system didn’t. I know I’m covered. I know if something serious happens, it will be handled properly. I’m not worried about the bill.
But I miss the responsiveness. In Turkey, healthcare felt human — someone would actually talk to you, quickly, and figure it out. Here, the system is excellent but sometimes feels like you’re a file in a queue.
For routine care and serious illness, I trust Germany. For speed and flexibility, Turkey wins.
Part of the Turkey vs Germany series.

content:
Turkey vs Germany: An Honest Comparison After Making the Move # Not a travel guide. Not a rant. Just what I’ve actually experienced living in both countries.
I want to be clear upfront: this isn’t about which country is “better.” It’s about what I personally found different, harder, easier, worse, and surprisingly better — from both sides. Because Germany has real frustrations too.
⚖️ Rule of Law & Justice # This was one of the biggest factors for me.
In Turkey, the law exists — but its application can feel inconsistent. Whether a rule applies to you sometimes seems to depend on who you know, who you are, or who’s watching. That unpredictability wears on you over time.
In Germany — and across much of Western Europe — the system isn’t perfect, but the rules tend to apply regardless of who you are. Guilty means guilty. The evidence matters. That consistency creates a kind of psychological safety that I didn’t fully appreciate until I had it.
🎓 Education Systems # Turkey’s education system has faced serious credibility challenges in recent years — including diploma fraud scandals that have forced honest graduates into the exhausting position of having to prove their degree is real when applying for jobs. That’s demoralizing in a way that’s hard to overstate.
Germany takes a different approach entirely. Not everyone is pushed toward university — and that’s intentional. Vocational training (Ausbildung) is taken seriously here. A two or three-year program combines classroom learning with real apprenticeship work: someone training to be a carpenter might spend half their week actually working in a carpenter’s workshop.
The result is a workforce where skilled trades are genuinely respected — and where someone who discovers a passion for learning later in life can still find their way into higher education. The system has room for second chances.
💶 Economic Stability # The Turkish lira has been losing value against foreign currencies for years. This isn’t just an abstract statistic — it means your savings shrink while you sleep, and long-term planning becomes a kind of gamble.
In Germany, inflation exists, but it’s measured in single digits. A salary of €3,000–5,000 a month covers a comfortable life. You can save, plan, and build — without watching the value of your work evaporate.
For anyone thinking about long-term financial security, this difference is enormous.
🛡️ Personal Safety & Accountability # In Turkey, I often felt that accidents happen and no one is held responsible. Road safety, construction quality, workplace standards — these are areas where enforcement is inconsistent and consequences for negligence can be minimal. The feeling that something could go wrong and nobody would be accountable was a low-level anxiety I carried for years.
Germany is far from perfect, but the accountability culture is real. If something goes wrong, there’s a process. There’s a responsible party. The system investigates. That doesn’t prevent all accidents — but it does create incentives to do things properly.
🐌 Bureaucracy: Germany’s Biggest Weakness # Here’s where Germany earns its criticism — and deserves it.
German bureaucracy is painfully slow. Need to register something? Submit documents in person, wait for a letter by post, come back in three weeks. The irony is that you sometimes hand over papers to someone who could check them immediately — and they still send everything by mail.
In Turkey, if there’s a problem, someone picks up the phone and fixes it. Customer service is fast, responsive, and often available around the clock.
I had a neighbor here whose internet stopped working. It took three days to get anyone on the phone — and that was a Sunday, so nothing happened until Monday. In Turkey, that gets resolved the same day.
Germany is efficient in systems. It is not always efficient in human responsiveness.
👮 Citizens as Enforcers # One thing that genuinely surprised me: in Germany, citizens actively enforce the rules on each other.
Park your car in the wrong spot? A neighbor may report you before the parking inspector even arrives. Planning renovations in your apartment? You don’t just need a permit from the municipality — you need to formally notify your neighbors and get their agreement.
This creates a culture of collective accountability that can feel intrusive if you’re not used to it. But it also means the rules actually get followed — not because the state is watching, but because everyone around you is.
🧭 Bottom Line # Topic Turkey Germany Rule of law Inconsistent Consistent Education University-focused, credibility issues Vocational + academic, both respected Economy High inflation, currency risk Stable, predictable Personal safety Accountability gaps Strong accountability culture Bureaucracy Fast, responsive Slow, postal-age processes Customer service Excellent Often frustrating Civic enforcement State-led Community-led Neither country is a utopia. But for me, for my family, for the future I wanted to build — the balance tipped clearly in one direction.
Your balance might look different. That’s okay. The point is to look at it honestly.
What’s your experience — have you lived in both countries, or made a similar comparison? I’d love to hear your perspective.

content:
Why I Left Turkey: The Decision That Changed Everything # A personal account of weighing family, freedom, and the future — from someone who actually made the move
Moving to another country is never just a practical decision. It’s emotional, messy, and deeply personal. I’ve been asked many times: “Why did you leave?” This is my honest answer.
🧳 I’d Done It Before # After university, I spent about a year living in England. I got a taste of life abroad — the independence, the new perspectives, the quiet satisfaction of figuring out a foreign city.
But I came back. Back then, family pulled harder than anything else. My parents were in Turkey. My roots were there. The idea of building a life far from them felt like a loss I wasn’t ready for.
So I returned. And for a while, that felt right.
👨‍👩‍👧 Then Everything Changed # Time passed. I got married. We had a child. And suddenly the question wasn’t “where do I want to live?” but “where do I want my child to grow up?”
Those are very different questions.
When I started looking at the world through my child’s eyes — the school system, the streets, the opportunities, the safety — my calculus shifted completely. The future stopped being abstract. It had a face.
👴 The Hardest Part: Leaving Family Behind # I won’t pretend this was easy. My parents, my extended family — they’re still in Turkey. They wanted us close. And a part of me will always feel the weight of that distance.
But I’ve come to believe that building a future for your children sometimes means making sacrifices in the present. Choosing the life you want for them, even when it costs you something real.
This doesn’t make the distance hurt less. It just makes it feel necessary.
🧒 The Children Argument # One of the things that pushed me most decisively was reading about child development in Northern Europe — particularly the Netherlands, but Germany too.
What struck me most: in the early school years, children aren’t given homework. The philosophy is that childhood itself is the education. Play, exploration, boredom even — these aren’t obstacles to development, they are development.
The result? The Netherlands consistently ranks among the happiest countries in the world, and their children among the happiest children. That’s not a coincidence.
I also noticed something on the streets of Munich that I couldn’t unsee: 7 and 8-year-olds cycling to school alone. No parent escort, no anxiety — just a child on a bike, navigating the city with confidence. That kind of independence, given early, shapes a person for life.
⚖️ What Tipped the Scale # It wasn’t one thing. It was the accumulation:
Watching the Turkish lira lose value year after year, and feeling the floor shift beneath my savings Worrying about road safety, construction standards, accountability — or the lack of it Seeing the education system struggle with trust and credibility Wanting my child to grow up in a place where the rules apply to everyone equally None of these made Turkey unlivable. But together, they made the question impossible to ignore.
🎯 My Advice to Anyone Considering This # Define your own criteria. Mine won’t be yours.
For some people, family proximity will always outweigh everything else — and that’s a completely valid choice. For others, economic stability or educational opportunity comes first. There’s no universal right answer.
What matters is that you ask the question honestly, without letting fear or inertia answer it for you.
I asked. And my answer led me to Munich.
What factors matter most to you when thinking about where to build a life? I’d genuinely like to know — drop a comment below.
