content:
Trust as a Way of Life: What Germany Taught Me About Believing in People # Living in Munich as an expat from Turkey — small details that changed how I see society
Germany surprised me. Not with its punctuality, not with its efficiency — but with something quieter and harder to name: trust. The kind of trust that doesn’t ask for proof. The kind that assumes you’ll do the right thing, even when no one is watching.
Here are the moments that made me feel it.
🚌 Public Transport: No Turnstiles, No Guards # When I first took the bus in Munich, I waited for the gate. There was no gate. No ticket inspector at the door, no obligation to board from the front. You buy your ticket — or you don’t — and you ride.
This isn’t a loophole. It’s a deliberate design choice. The system trusts that most people are honest, and that the cost of treating everyone like a potential cheater is greater than the cost of occasional free riders. Coming from a country where every bus has a driver watching every passenger, this felt almost radical.
📰 Unmanned Newspaper Stands # At street corners across Germany, you’ll find wooden racks filled with newspapers. No vendor. No camera. No lock.
You take what you want, leave the money, and walk away. The math works — not because Germans are saints, but because the social contract is taken seriously. Defecting from it carries its own weight.
🥕 Farm Stalls Without Farmers # Drive through the countryside around Munich and you’ll spot them: small wooden tables by the road, loaded with tomatoes, carrots, or potatoes — portioned, priced, unattended. A handwritten sign. A tin can for coins.
No one watching. No security. Just produce and a price.
These Hofläden (farm stalls) are quietly one of the most powerful symbols of civic trust I’ve encountered. They only work if people let them work — and somehow, they do.
🏠 The Honest Utility Bill # In Germany, rent often comes in two forms: Kaltmiete (cold rent — the base) and Warmmiete (warm rent — including heating and utilities). At the end of the year, landlords and tenants sit down together and calculate actual usage.
Who used more hot water? Who kept the heating on all winter? The adjustment is made honestly, based on real numbers — sometimes you owe more, sometimes you get money back.
No one is surveilled. The assumption is honesty, not suspicion.
🏖️ Toys Left Outside All Summer # Almost every apartment building in Munich has a small playground — a slide, a swing, a sandbox. And in that sandbox, all summer long, you’ll find children’s toys: buckets, shovels, toy trucks, sometimes even bikes left leaning against the fence overnight.
No one takes them.
I watched this for weeks before I believed it. These toys belong to the children of the building, but in a sense they belong to all the children. No fence needed. No lock. Just shared space, shared respect.
🧤 Lost and Found — On the Street # Drop a glove in Munich. Come back an hour later. It will likely still be there — or hanging on a nearby railing, placed carefully by a stranger so you can spot it when you retrace your steps.
I’ve seen this with gloves, scarves, keys, headphones. Once, with a phone.
It’s such a small act, but it speaks to something large: the instinct here isn’t “this is unclaimed, I could take it” — it’s “this belongs to someone, I’ll make it easier for them to find.”
💬 What This Means # I’m not saying Germany is perfect. I’m not saying this is unique to Germany. And I know that trust at this scale is partly a product of systems, history, and demographics that are complicated.
But living here — in Munich, day to day — I feel it. A low-level sense of safety that isn’t just about crime statistics. It’s about walking through a city that assumes the best of you, and somehow making you want to live up to it.
That’s a rare thing. And it’s worth noticing.
Have you experienced something similar — or the opposite — in another country? I’d love to hear it in the comments.

content:
Regensburg: Germany’s Best-Preserved Medieval City # Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2006, Regensburg has preserved its medieval texture to a remarkable degree — one of Europe’s rarest examples. About 1,500 historic buildings, most from the Middle Ages, all still in use today.
Tower houses built by Italian merchants give the city a skyline unlike any other German city.
Getting There # Train: Regional Express from Munich Hauptbahnhof, ~1.5–2 hours. Deutschlandticket valid.
Places to Visit # 1. Steinerne Brücke (Stone Bridge) # Regensburg’s symbol. Dating from the 12th century over the Danube — once Europe’s longest stone bridge. Still open to pedestrians. From the bridge, the cathedral’s twin towers and city skyline are incredible.
Tip: Early morning or sunset — the bridge looks different at both times.
2. Dom St. Peter (Cathedral) # Bavaria’s most important Gothic cathedral. Started in the 13th century, completed in the 19th. Massive interior — stained glass, sculptural details and acoustics are impressive. Free entry.
Regensburg Domspatzen: The cathedral’s associated boys’ choir, one of the world’s oldest. Hearing them at Sunday services is a special experience.
3. Porta Praetoria # Roman-era northern gate. From 179 AD. Found in a shop’s backyard — visible from inside. A direct trace of Rome.
4. Italian Tower Houses (Geschlechtertürme) # In the Middle Ages, wealthy merchant families built towers as symbols of power and prestige. About 20 of these towers still stand in Regensburg. Baumburger Turm and Goldener Turm are must-sees.
These tower houses create a skyline similar to Italian medieval cities (like San Gimignano) — nothing like it in Germany.
5. Altstadt Walk # Street by street, walking is an experience in itself. Every corner holds a historic building, every building a centuries-old story. No tourist crowds — real city life going on.
6. Danube Riverside # Both banks of the Steinerne Brücke along the riverside. In summer locals picnic and swim here. Lively atmosphere.
Historische Wurstküche: Right beside the Stone Bridge, a historic sausage kitchen operating in the same spot since the 12th century. Crowded but at least get a sausage and eat it by the river.
7. Schloss Thurn und Taxis # Giant palace open as museum inside the city. Built over a Carolingian-era monastery. Interior tour ~15 EUR but impressive.
Food # Historische Wurstküche — mandatory stop. Small, historic, by the river. Sausage + mustard + Sauerkraut. Long queue possible but worth it.
Regensburg is more affordable than Munich — many options around the Altstadt.
Practical Information # How long? 5–6 hours to comfortably see the city. Add Schloss Thurn und Taxis for a full day.
Stroller: Altstadt is largely cobblestone — difficult for strollers. Danube riverside and wider streets are good.
Best season: Spring and autumn are best. Summer has crowds but not as intense as Munich.
FAQ # How to get to Regensburg from Munich? Regional Express, ~1.5 hours. Deutschlandticket valid.
Can Regensburg be seen in one day? Yes, a full day is plenty — leave early morning, return in the evening.
Is Regensburg UNESCO? Yes, UNESCO World Heritage since 2006 — the Altstadt and Stadtamhof.
Does Historische Wurstküche need a reservation? No. Queue up and go. Eaten outside by the river.
summary: Regensburg is Germany’s best-preserved medieval city and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Steinerne Brücke, a massive cathedral, Italian-style tower houses and the Danube riverbank — a world apart. 1.5 hours from Munich.
title: Regensburg Travel Guide | UNESCO Medieval City 1.5 Hours from Munich (2025)

content:
Ingolstadt: Beyond Audi # When people hear Ingolstadt, Audi comes to mind — the headquarters is here. But the historic center tells a completely different story: the Danube River, medieval walls and unexpected museums make for a small but rich city.
1 hour from Munich, free with Deutschlandticket.
Getting There # Train: Regional Express from Munich Hauptbahnhof, ~1 hour. Deutschlandticket valid. Frequent departures.
Places to Visit # 1. Altstadt and City Walls # Ingolstadt’s medieval walls are largely preserved. You can walk along them. Kreuztor — the city’s most beautiful gate, 14th century.
2. Neues Schloss (New Palace) and Army Museum # Named “new” but dating from the 15th century. Inside is the Bayerisches Armeemuseum (Bavarian Army Museum) — one of Germany’s largest military museums. Entry ~5 EUR.
3. Liebfrauenmünster (Church) # Ingolstadt’s most important religious building. 14th–15th century Gothic church. The frescoes and stained glass inside are impressive.
4. Danube (Donau) Riverside # The Danube flows beside the city. Walking and cycling paths along the bank. Green spaces, picnic spots. The city’s most peaceful and beautiful section.
5. The Frankenstein Connection # An interesting historical detail: Mary Shelley chose Ingolstadt University as the place where Doctor Frankenstein worked when writing Frankenstein. There are signs and small monuments around the city referencing this connection.
6. Audi Forum (Optional) # ~10 minutes from the city center. Car museum, factory tours (advance booking required). Interesting for car enthusiasts but not essential for a city visit.
Practical Information # How long? 3–4 hours for the center. Add Audi Forum for 5–6 hours.
Stroller: Center largely flat and modern. Danube riverside excellent. Historic Altstadt partially cobblestone.
FAQ # How to get to Ingolstadt from Munich? Regional Express, ~1 hour. Deutschlandticket valid.
Is the Audi factory tour free? Museum is free but factory tour is paid and requires booking.
How long to spend in Ingolstadt? Half a day for the city. Full day possible.
summary: Ingolstadt is known as Audi’s headquarters but its historic center is far richer. A city on the Danube River with medieval walls, the Bavarian Army Museum and an unexpected Frankenstein connection. 1 hour from Munich.
title: Ingolstadt Travel Guide | Danube City 1 Hour from Munich (2025)

content:
Neuschwanstein: Real or Fairy Tale? # Seeing it for the first time is hard to believe. Clinging to a mountainside in the middle of the Alps — pink and white, like fiction. But it was completed in 1886, by order of the eccentric King Ludwig II.
Disney used this castle as inspiration for Sleeping Beauty’s palace. But Disney’s copy can’t surpass the original.
Town guide separately: Füssen Town Guide →
Getting There # Train: Munich → Füssen, Regional Express, ~2 hours. Deutschlandticket valid.
From Füssen to the castle:
Bus: Lines 73 or 78 to Schwangau Schlösser stop. ~10 min. Taxi: Direct from Füssen station. From Schwangau to the castle:
Walking: ~30–40 minutes, uphill Horse carriage: Ticketed, long queues in busy seasons Shuttle bus: Takes you close to the top Tickets: The Most Critical Point # Buy online in advance — not just a cliché here, genuinely essential. Daily visitor numbers are limited. In summer, days can sell out well in advance.
Where: www.ticket-center-hohenschwangau.de
Price (2025): Adult ~15 EUR, children (0–17) free.
Booking fee: 2.50 EUR extra for online — worth it.
Important: You must arrive at the ticket counter at the time specified on your ticket. Being 5 minutes late for your tour time means your ticket is invalid.
Inside the Castle Tour # Ticket includes: guided interior tour, approximately 35 minutes. Audio guide available in multiple languages.
Highlights: Throne Room (Tronsaal), bedroom and study, Grotto (artificial cave), Knights’ Hall.
Ludwig II could only use this castle for 172 nights before his death in 1886.
Marienbrücke: Best Viewpoint # The bridge near the castle — this is actually where you need to go for photos. From the bridge you can see both the castle and the valley — the classic Neuschwanstein photos you see online are taken from here.
How to get there: 10 minutes walk from the castle, uphill.
Warning: Bridge may be closed in winter. Check before going.
Tip: Go to Marienbrücke early morning — cloudless sky and fewer people.
Tips to Avoid Crowds # 1.5 million visitors per year. Overwhelming crowds at summer midday. To avoid:
Book the first tour slot — opens at 09:00, first tours are quietest Prefer weekdays — weekends much more crowded Visit in April or October — not as crowded as summer Walk up instead of taking the bus — skip the bus queue Hohenschwangau Castle: The Other Option # Directly opposite Neuschwanstein, on the lower hill — another castle, Hohenschwangau. Ludwig II spent his childhood here. Less crowded, much more authentic interior.
Sold as a combined package ticket with Neuschwanstein. See it if you have time.
With Stroller # Castle interior cannot be visited with a stroller — they don’t allow them inside. Leave it at the ticket office (free storage). The uphill walk to the castle is steep — horse carriage or shuttle bus is a sensible option.
Practical Information # How long? Castle tour 35 minutes. Allow 4–5 hours including travel and waiting. Add Marienbrücke: 5–6 hours.
Food: Small shops near the castle entrance. No large restaurant — bring food.
Best season: May–June and September–October. July–August most crowded.
FAQ # Can Neuschwanstein tickets sell out? Yes, in summer they can sell out days in advance. Buy online.
Do children pay? Ages 0–17 are free.
Is overnight stay needed? Not for just the castle. Day trip from Munich works if you leave early.
Best spot for castle photos? Marienbrücke bridge — the classic shot.
summary: Neuschwanstein is Bavaria’s and probably Germany’s most visited structure — 1.5 million visitors per year. When to go and how to avoid crowds, how to buy tickets — 2025 practical guide.
title: Neuschwanstein Castle Guide | Disney's Inspiration | Practical Tips (2025)

content:
Füssen: The City Living in Neuschwanstein’s Shadow # Everyone comes to Füssen for Neuschwanstein — but leaves without seeing Füssen itself. This article is for the city: Hohes Schloss, the Altstadt, the Lech River and surroundings.
Castle guide separately: Neuschwanstein Castle | Disney’s Inspiration →
Getting There # Train: Direct Regional Express from Munich Hauptbahnhof. ~2 hours. Deutschlandticket valid.
Places to Visit # 1. Hohes Schloss (High Castle) # On the hill overlooking the city. The most important structure in Füssen for those not going to Neuschwanstein. 15th-century summer residence of Bavarian princes.
Fascinating detail: For someone looking from outside, you can’t tell the windows on the walls aren’t real — all windows are painted using trompe l’œil (optical illusion) technique. Very impressive.
Art gallery inside (Staatsgalerie). Entry ~6 EUR. Outside free.
2. Altstadt (Old Town) # Right below Hohes Schloss. Colorful facade houses, narrow streets, historic buildings. Better preserved than most Bavarian small towns.
3. Lech River Canyon (Lechhöhlen) # The Lech River running right beside the city has created a small canyon here. Looking down from the bridge, the turquoise water and rocks are incredible. Free, easy access.
4. Forggensee (Lake Forggen) # Artificial lake 2 km south of Füssen. Great for swimming in summer. Lakeside cycling and walking paths. Alps and Neuschwanstein in the background — ideal photo spot.
Tip: The best angle to photograph Neuschwanstein is from Forggensee.
Practical Information # How long? 2–3 hours for the city. Add Forggensee or Neuschwanstein for a full day.
Stroller: Altstadt partly cobblestone but manageable. Forggensee lakeside excellent.
FAQ # Can Füssen town be visited independently of Neuschwanstein? Absolutely. 2–3 beautiful hours just for the city.
Is Forggensee close to Füssen? Yes, ~2 km. Easy by foot or bike.
Is an overnight stay needed in Füssen? Not for just the city. A full day covers Neuschwanstein + town + Forggensee.
summary: Everyone goes to Füssen for Neuschwanstein — but leaves without seeing the town itself. This guide is for the city: Hohes Schloss with its trompe l’œil windows, the Altstadt, the Lech River canyon and nearby Forggensee.
title: Füssen Travel Guide | Alpine Medieval Town Beyond Neuschwanstein (2025)

content:
Lindau: An Island City in the Middle of a Lake # On the Bavarian side of Lake Constance (Bodensee), built on a small island connected to the mainland by a bridge. The moment you step into the old town, the lake surrounds you and Austrian mountains rise across the water.
This view exists nowhere else.
Getting There # Train: Direct Regional Express from Munich Hauptbahnhof. About 2 hours. Deutschlandticket valid. The station is on the island — you’re already in the center.
Places to Visit # 1. Harbor (Hafen) and Lion Statue # Lindau’s most iconic image: the Bavarian lion statue at the harbor entrance, opposite the lighthouse. Austrian mountains in the background. Perfect for photos — less crowded early morning or evening.
Boat rental and lake tours available (separately priced).
2. Altstadt (Old Town) # Colorful houses with balconies, flower-decorated narrow streets. Medieval texture almost entirely preserved. Walking around is an experience in itself.
3. Mangturm (Mang Tower) # Medieval lighthouse tower, symbol of the harbor. Particularly beautiful in late afternoon sun.
4. Lake Walk # Circling the island takes about 30–40 minutes. Lake views from every direction, Austrian and Swiss mountains visible. Excellent for strollers — completely flat.
5. Swimming in Lake Constance # In summer, the lake is great for swimming. Beaches around the island. Very clean water.
Three Country Feature # Lindau is very close to where three countries share Lake Constance — Germany, Austria and Switzerland. Bregenz (Austria) is just across the lake — very close by boat.
Practical Information # How long? 3–4 hours for the island. Full day possible adding lake tours or Bregenz.
Stroller: Island completely flat — ideal. Harbor walk, Altstadt, surroundings — all comfortable.
Best season: May–September. Summer can be crowded. Spring and autumn quieter.
FAQ # How to get to Lindau from Munich? Regional Express direct, ~2 hours. Deutschlandticket valid.
Can you cross to Austria from Lindau? Yes. Bregenz is very close by boat or train. Extra ticket needed.
Is there swimming in Lindau? Yes, several swimming spots around the lake in summer.
summary: Lindau is a small island city on Lake Constance (Bodensee) in Bavaria, neighboring Austria and Switzerland. Harbor lion, colorful houses, lake views. 2 hours from Munich with Deutschlandticket.
title: Lindau Travel Guide | Island City on Lake Constance | 2 Hours from Munich (2025)

content:
Augsburg: The Unjustly Overlooked City # Most visitors to Munich skip Augsburg. Yet just 35 minutes away, a city with a far longer history — over 2,000 years of Roman heritage — is waiting.
Fuggerei alone is reason enough to come: the world’s oldest social housing project, in continuous operation since 1516.
Getting There # Train: Direct from Munich Hauptbahnhof, 30–40 minutes. Deutschlandticket valid. Multiple departures per hour.
Places to Visit # 1. Fuggerei — World’s Oldest Social Housing Project # Built in 1516 by Jakob Fugger for the poor of Augsburg — and still used for the same purpose today. Residents pay just 88 cents a year (the historical 1 Rhenish guilden). In return, they pray three times a day in the church — for the Fugger family.
Visit: Small museum open. WWII air raid shelter visible. Entry ~4 EUR.
2. Rathaus and Goldener Saal # Augsburg’s symbol. 17th-century Renaissance masterpiece. Inside, the Goldener Saal (Golden Hall) is stunning: ceiling decorations, gold leaf, magnificent proportions. Destroyed in WWII, fully restored.
3. Augsburger Dom (Cathedral) # One of Germany’s oldest churches. 11th century, with foundations going back even further. Most notable: world’s oldest figurative stained glass windows — 11th century, still original.
4. Maximilianstrasse # Augsburg’s main street, called one of Europe’s most beautiful. Fugger palaces, grand churches and three large fountains (Mercury, Hercules, Augustus) line this street.
5. Water Management System # UNESCO World Heritage. Medieval water canal system. Canals visible throughout the city.
Practical Information # How long? 4–5 hours for the center including Fuggerei.
Stroller: Center largely flat and comfortable. Canal areas have some cobblestones but generally good.
Cheaper than Munich: Rent is about half of Munich’s. Many Munich workers live in Augsburg.
FAQ # How to get to Augsburg from Munich? S-Bahn (S3) or Regional Express, 30–40 minutes. Deutschlandticket valid.
Where is Fuggerei? In the Jakobervorstadt neighborhood, 10–15 minutes walk from center.
Is one day enough for Augsburg? Yes, a full day is perfect. Leave early morning, return in the evening.
summary: Augsburg is a historic city 35 minutes from Munich with over 2,000 years of Roman history, and home to the world’s oldest social housing project — the Fuggerei, still operating since 1516.
title: Augsburg Travel Guide | Roman City 35 Minutes from Munich (2025)

content:
Salzburg: Munich’s Nearest Foreign City # From Munich, Salzburg feels almost like a neighbor. A 1.5-hour train ride, free with Deutschlandticket to the border. A few euros more for the short stretch after Freilassing.
But when you arrive, you’re in another country — Austria, with all its cultural weight: Mozart, Hohensalzburg and Sound of Music.
Getting There # Train: Munich Hauptbahnhof → Freilassing (free with Deutschlandticket) → Salzburg Hauptbahnhof (separate ticket, ~4–6 EUR). Total ~1.5 hours. Direct service, no change needed — brief stop at Freilassing.
Places to Visit # 1. Hohensalzburg Fortress # Dominating the city from atop its hill, one of Europe’s largest and best-preserved medieval fortresses. Dating from the 11th century.
How to get up: Walk (~20 min, uphill) or Funicular from Kapitel Square — sold as a package with fortress ticket. Funicular + fortress museum ~16 EUR adult.
View: Salzburg city, Salzach River and surrounding Alps — magnificent.
2. Getreidegasse # Salzburg’s most famous street. Narrow, charming, decorated with the iron guild signs. Mozart was born here — Mozart Geburtshaus at Getreidegasse 9. Museum entry ~13 EUR.
Tip: Very crowded. Go early morning or late afternoon.
3. Mirabell Palace and Gardens # Free entry. Baroque gardens, manicured flower beds, views of Hohensalzburg. The “Do-Re-Mi” scene from Sound of Music was filmed here. Perfect for photos.
4. Altstadt (Old Town) # UNESCO World Heritage. Religious buildings, narrow streets, Residenzplatz. Salzburger Dom (Cathedral) is a must — the interior is incredible.
5. Kapuzinerberg # Small hill opposite Mirabell. Worth climbing for panoramic city views — free, 15–20 minute walk.
Practical Information # Currency: Euro. Language: German, English very common in tourist areas.
How long? Full day — leave early morning, return in the evening. 5–7 hours on the ground is plenty.
Stroller: Altstadt partially cobblestone. Mirabell gardens perfect. Fortress accessible by funicular.
Best season: April–October. July–August very crowded. December Christmas markets are wonderful.
FAQ # Visa for Salzburg? Schengen visa sufficient. Austria is in Schengen.
Is Deutschlandticket valid to Salzburg? Valid to Freilassing. Freilassing → Salzburg separate ticket (~4–6 EUR). Total very cheap.
Is one day enough for Salzburg? Yes for the city. Add Hallstatt or Salzkammergut for 2 days.
summary: Salzburg is a 1.5-hour train journey from Munich — Mozart’s birthplace and the city where Sound of Music was filmed. Deutschlandticket takes you to the border free — a few euros more and you’re in Austria.
title: Salzburg Travel Guide | Mozart's City 1.5 Hours from Munich (2025)

content:
Memmingen: Bavaria’s Hidden Medieval Gem # When people think of medieval Bavaria, Rothenburg ob der Tauber comes to mind. But there’s a far less touristy, far more authentic alternative: Memmingen.
The city walls are almost entirely intact. Historic buildings surrounding the Marktplatz have kept their original character. No tourist crowds — so you can actually feel the city.
From Munich by Deutschlandticket: 1.5 hours, free.
Getting There # Train: Regional Express direct from Munich Hauptbahnhof. About 1 hour 20–30 minutes. Deutschlandticket valid.
Station to city center: 10–15 minutes on foot. Flat and easy.
Places to Visit # 1. Marktplatz # The heart of the city. A wide square surrounded by historic buildings. In the center is a fountain, alongside the Steuerhaus (tax house) — dating from the 15th century, the city’s most beautiful building with incredible carved facade details.
A weekly market is held here with local products, fruit, vegetables and cheese.
2. Stadtmauer (City Walls) # What makes Memmingen special: the walls are largely still standing. You can walk along them and look out over the city from the towers. Total walk ~45 minutes, not strenuous.
3. Martinskirche (St. Martin’s Church) # Right next to the square. Late Gothic style, dating from the 14th century. Interior is quite impressive — especially the wooden carved choir section. Free entry.
4. Sieben Dächer (Seven Roofs) District # Memmingen’s most photographed spot. Seven wooden structures leaning against each other over a small canal. Medieval, once used by leather craftsmen. Now fully restored, very photogenic.
5. Weberhaus (Weavers’ House) # On the corner of Marktplatz, 16th-century home of the weavers’ guild. Frescoes still vivid.
Practical Information # How long? 3–4 hours is sufficient. Leave after breakfast, return by afternoon.
Stroller: City center is cobblestone. Some wall sections tricky. Center is manageable but not perfect.
Best time: Weekdays — weekends with the market are nice too.
FAQ # How to get to Memmingen from Munich? Regional Express direct, ~1.5 hours. Deutschlandticket valid.
How long to spend in Memmingen? 3–4 hours for the city. Add Allgäu region for a full day.
Is Memmingen expensive? Much more affordable than Munich. Small city, no tourist prices.
summary: Memmingen is one of Bavaria’s least-known but best-preserved medieval towns. Near-complete city walls, Marktplatz, half-timbered buildings — a small but complete open-air museum. 1.5 hours from Munich with Deutschlandticket.
title: Memmingen Travel Guide | Medieval City 1.5 Hours from Munich (2025)

content:
Almanya’da Yılbaşı: Silvester # Almanya’da yılbaşına Silvester deniyor — 31 Aralık akşamından 1 Ocak’a geçilen gece. İsim, Katolik takviminde 31 Aralık’a denk gelen Papa Silvester I’den geliyor.
Türkiye’deki yılbaşı kutlamalarından çok farklı bir atmosfer. Dışarıya adım attığınızda anlıyorsunuz: havai fişek özgürlüğü bu ülkede gerçek anlamıyla var.
Havai Fişek: Herkes Kendi Atıyor # Almanya’da 28–31 Aralık tarihleri arasında havai fişek satışı serbest bırakılıyor. Süpermarketlerde, benzin istasyonlarında, özel dükkanlarında onlarca çeşit havai fişek satılıyor.
31 Aralık gecesi saat 00:00’ı geçtiğinde şehrin üzeri sis gibi duman oluyor. Her sokaktan, her balkondan, her bahçeden aynı anda fişekler yükseliyor. Örgütlü bir gösteri değil — tamamen kaotik ve aynı zamanda inanılmaz.
İlk Silvester’imizden not: Münih’in güneydoğusunda yaşıyoruz. Gece 00:00’da çocuklar uyurken balkona çıktık. Her yönden fişek, her renk, bazen çok yakın. 20 dakika boyunca şehrin tamamı ateş altındaymış gibi. İmkansız olduğunu düşündüğünüz bir şey.
Havai Fişek Hukuku # Satış: 28–31 Aralık Kullanım: yalnızca 31 Aralık gecesi ve 1 Ocak sabahı (gece yarısı etrafında) Bazı şehirler ve bölgeler yasak uygulayabiliyor — yerel kuralları kontrol edin Yangın riski olan ormanlık alanlarda her zaman yasak Silvester Gelenekleri # Bleigiessen (Kurşun Dökmek) # Erimiş metal (artık kurşun yerine mum balmumu kullanılıyor — güvenlik nedeniyle) soğuk suya dökülüyor. Oluşan şeklin geleceği tahmin ettiğine inanılıyor. Her şekle bir anlam var: çapa = umut, yıldız = başarı, kalp = aşk…
Süpermarketlerde Silvester öncesi “Bleigießen” setleri satılıyor. Çocuklarla eğlenceli bir aktivite.
Dinner for One # Her yıl 31 Aralık’ta Almanya televizyonlarında onlarca kez yayınlanan, 1963 yapımı İngilizce bir kısa film. Neredeyse hiçbir İngiliz bilmez ama her Alman ezberden bilir. Silvester’ın gayri resmi ulusal filmi.
Raclette ve Fondue # Silvester akşam yemeği için en yaygın iki seçenek. Raclette makinesiyle masada peynir eritilir, üstüne sebze ve patates konur. Saatlerce süren, sosyal bir yemek ritüeli. Fondue ise peynir veya et bazlı olabilir.
Süpermarketler Aralık ayında Raclette malzemelerini ön plana alıyor — kolayca bulursunuz.
Münih’te Yılbaşı Kutlama Seçenekleri # Olimpiyat Parkı (Olympiapark) # Her yıl büyük Silvester etkinliği düzenleniyor. Canlı konser, havai fişek gösterisi ve binlerce kişi. Bilet gerekiyor — önceden alın.
Marienplatz # Şehir merkezinin kalbi. Yılbaşında kalabalık toplanıyor ama resmi bir program yok — insanlar kendiliğinden kutluyor. Meydana çıkan herkesin elinde şampanya.
İngiliz Bahçesi (Englischer Garten) # Daha sakin bir atmosfer isteyenler için. Bazı gruplar burada toplanıyor.
Evde Kutlama # Almanlar’ın önemli bir kısmı aile veya arkadaşlarla evde kutluyor. Raclette yeniyor, Dinner for One izleniyor, gece yarısı balkona çıkılıp fişek atılıyor veya izleniyor.
Çocuklarla Silvester # Küçük çocuklar için yılbaşı gecesi zor olabilir — gürültü, geç saat, uyku düzeni bozuluyor.
Bizim çözümümüz: Çocukları normal saatte yatırdık. Saat 23:45’te uyandırdık, balkona çıktık, birlikte izledik. 1 Ocak 00:15’te tekrar yatırdık. İkisi de heyecanla izledi — büyük bir bozulma olmadı.
Alternatif: Çocuklar için erken mini kutlama yapılabiliyor — gece 18:00’de “çocuk yılbaşı” yapan aileler var. Pasta, balonlar, erken fişek.
Uyarı: Büyük havai fişek sesleri küçük çocukları ve hayvanları korkutabiliyor. Köpek sahipleri için Silvester gecesi zor.
Pratik Bilgiler # Toplu taşıma: Silvester gecesi MVV ücretsiz (Münih). Pek çok şehirde gece servisi uzatılıyor — kontrol edin.
Restoran rezervasyonu: İyi restoranlar Silvester için özel menü yapıyor ve çok önceden doluyor. Dışarıda yemek planı varsa Aralık başında rezervasyon yapın.
Alışveriş: 31 Aralık’ta marketler erken kapanıyor. Malzemelerinizi önceden alın.
1 Ocak: Resmi tatil. Her yer kapalı — marketler dahil. Hazırlıklı olun.
Türkiye ile Karşılaştırma # Türkiye Almanya Yılbaşı adı Yılbaşı / Noel (karışık) Silvester Kutlama yeri Genelde evde veya restoran Sokak + ev karışık Havai fişek Kısıtlı, merkezi Herkes kendi atıyor Yemek Hindi, börek Raclette, Fondue Gelenek Çam ağacı, Noel Baba Bleigiessen, Dinner for One Süpermarket 1 Ocak açık 1 Ocak kapalı Sık Sorulan Sorular # Almanya’da yılbaşı mı Noel mi daha önemli? Kesinlikle Noel (Weihnachten) — 24–26 Aralık aile için en önemli dönem. Silvester daha sosyal, dışa dönük.
Silvester için Münih’te nereye gidilir? Olympiapark etkinliği için bilet alın. Ya da Marienplatz’a gidin — ücretsiz, kalabalık, eğlenceli.
Havai fişek nereden alınır? Aralık sonunda tüm büyük marketler (Rewe, Edeka, Aldi, Lidl) ve özel dükkanlar satıyor.
Almanya’da yılbaşı ağacı ne zaman kaldırılır? Geleneksel olarak 6 Ocak (Heilige Drei Könige) — Üç Kral Günü. Bazı aileler daha erken kaldırıyor.
summary: Almanya’da yılbaşı — Silvester — Türkiye’deki kutlamalardan çok farklı. Herkes kendi havai fişeğini atıyor, sokaklar duman içinde, Bleigiessen geleneği var. Münih’te ilk Silvester’imizden notlar.
title: Almanya'da Yılbaşı Kutlamaları | Silvester, Havai Fişekler ve Münih'te Yeni Yıl (2025)

content:
Christmas in Germany: Two Separate Celebrations # In many countries “New Year’s” means December 31st. In Germany, winter celebrations are split into two distinct periods:
Weihnachten (Christmas) — December 24-26: Family gatherings, quiet celebration, gift-giving. Germany’s most important holiday of the year.
Silvester — December 31st: New Year’s Eve. Fireworks, champagne, friend gatherings.
The two carry very different energy. And for someone who has just moved to Germany, both come with surprises.
Weihnachten: German Christmas Culture # Christmas Is a Family Holiday # In Germany, Christmas is entirely family-focused. December 24th evening (Heiligabend — Holy Night) is the most important moment. Dinner, opening gifts and usually church. Most restaurants and shops are closed.
For newcomers, this can be a surprise: the city essentially empties out from the afternoon of December 24th. Supermarkets close, metro runs less frequently.
Practical tip: Finish your shopping on December 23rd. December 24th midday is the last chance.
Advent: The Build-Up to Christmas # In Germany, Christmas doesn’t start on December 24th — it starts with Advent. Advent covers the four weeks before Christmas.
Adventskranz: A wreath with four candles. One candle is lit each Sunday. Adventskalender: A calendar with a daily surprise. Chocolate versions are very popular for children.
Munich Christmas Markets: Christkindlmarkt # Munich’s Christmas market is one of the most famous in the world. The main market at Marienplatz has been running since 1310.
Main markets:
Market Location Character Christkindlmarkt Marienplatz Largest, most traditional Mittelaltermarkt Wittelsbacherplatz Medieval theme Schwabing Markt Münchner Freiheit More local, calmer Haidhauser Markt Weißenburger Platz Hipster, alternative Tollwood Olympiapark Festival atmosphere, organic Opening dates: Usually late November to December 24th.
What to Eat and Drink at the Christmas Market? # Glühwein: Hot spiced wine. Can be red or white. In Munich, the mug price is usually 4-6 EUR (including mug deposit).
Kinderpunsch: Non-alcoholic Glühwein version, perfect for children.
Lebkuchen: Christmas spice cookies. The large heart-shaped ones make great souvenirs.
Bratwurst: Grilled sausage with mustard. A Christmas market essential.
Gebrannte Mandeln: Caramelized almonds. Their aroma fills the market.
Christmas Market with a Stroller # During busy periods (weekends, especially the last week of December) the main Marienplatz market gets very crowded. Pushing a stroller can become difficult.
Recommendation: Go on a weekday morning or during the first week of the market — both calmer and more atmospheric.
Silvester: A Night Beyond Expectations # For someone experiencing Silvester (December 31st) in Germany for the first time, the biggest surprise is: fireworks.
In Germany, individual fireworks sales are permitted for Silvester. From midnight onwards — and even before — fireworks are launched from everywhere. Looking out the window, the city seems to turn into a war zone.
This is very different from New Year’s celebrations elsewhere. Louder, more chaotic, more unexpected.
Where to Celebrate Silvester in Munich? # Marienplatz: Thousands gather, fireworks display at midnight. Englischer Garten: More free-spirited, picnic atmosphere. City bars and restaurants: Reservation essential, usually special menus and prices.
Celebrating at home: Many German families celebrate Silvester at home with close friends. Making Raclette is very popular — a tabletop grill with cheese and various toppings. A slow and enjoyable evening.
If You Have Pets # Silvester fireworks affect pets most of all. In Germany, this is a genuine problem. Veterinarians can prescribe sedatives in advance. If you have a pet, be prepared.
Christmas Shopping # Gifts at the Weihnachtsmarkt: Handmade products, toys, decorations. Prices are high but quality is good.
Post-Christmas sales: From December 26th, big sales start in Germany. Shopping after Christmas is very sensible.
Online shopping: Amazon.de gets very slow in November-December. Order during Black Friday to ensure delivery by Christmas.
Practical Information # Public holidays:
December 24 (Heiligabend): Half day December 25-26: Official holiday, most things closed December 31 (Silvester): Half day January 1: Official holiday Public transport: Runs infrequently on Christmas days (December 25-26) and January 1st. Plan ahead.
Silvester taxis: The hardest to find and most expensive night of the year. Celebrate somewhere walkable or book well in advance.
Frequently Asked Questions # How many days is the Christmas holiday in Germany? December 25-26 are official holidays. Many workplaces also close from December 24th afternoon.
When does the Munich Christmas market open? Usually from the last week of November to December 24th. Exact dates are announced each year.
Is there a non-alcoholic version of Glühwein? Yes, it’s available as Kinderpunsch or alkoholfreier Glühwein.
Can I buy fireworks for Silvester? Yes, they go on sale between December 28-31. However, some areas have restrictions — check your local area.
Are there places to visit in Munich on Christmas Day? Most museums and places are closed December 24-26. The Christmas market is open until midday on December 24th. The Englischer Garten is always open.
summary: Christmas in Germany is very different from what you might expect. Weihnachten is quiet and family-focused; Silvester is surprisingly loud. From Munich’s Christmas markets to last year’s Silvester experience — German winter culture through expat eyes.
title: Christmas and New Year in Germany | Munich Christmas Markets & Silvester (2025)

What Is the Deutschlandticket? # A subscription that started in 2023 and changed the lives of Germany residents: for 49 EUR per month, you can use all local and regional public transport. S-Bahn, U-Bahn, tram, bus, regional trains — all included.
What’s NOT included:
ICE and IC trains (high-speed) Private bus lines Austria and Switzerland (ends at the border — but you can reach nearby cities very cheaply) For someone Munich-based, this ticket is an incredible tool for exploring the surroundings. We tested this with two kids aged 1 and 5, and that famous double stroller.
Deutschlandticket with Children # How many children travel free? Children under 6 are free across Germany — no Deutschlandticket needed. Ages 6–14 in Bavaria can often travel free with Bavarian tickets — but this rule varies with the Deutschlandticket, so check.
Stroller: Generally no problem on regional trains and S-Bahn — special wagons available. But some smaller stations have no elevator. Research routes beforehand.
Top 7 Day Trip Routes from Munich # 1. Füssen and Neuschwanstein Castle # Duration: ~2 hours (Regional Express, direct) Cost: Free with Deutschlandticket
Bavaria’s and perhaps Germany’s most iconic destination. The pink and white turreted castle in front of mountain scenery. The castle that inspired Disney.
Practical info:
Buy entry tickets online in advance — daily visitor limit applies Bus from Füssen station to the castle (not included, separate ticket) For strollers: inside the castle is stepped, outside is fine Go early morning — overwhelming crowds at midday How many hours with kids? Full day — 8–10 hours
2. Garmisch-Partenkirchen # Duration: ~1.5 hours (direct) Cost: Free with Deutschlandticket
At the foot of Germany’s highest mountain, the Zugspitze. Winter ski resort, summer hiking routes.
Things to do:
Partnach Gorge (Partnachklamm) — mountain valley walk, very impressive Zugspitze summit (separately priced, 50–60 EUR/person) Walk around the town center For strollers: OK up to the Partnachklamm entrance — you can’t enter inside, but the riverside walk is an alternative.
3. Starnberg Lake (Starnberger See) # Duration: ~30 minutes (S6 line) Cost: Free with Deutschlandticket
Munich’s closest day trip destination. Walk from the station to the lake. Perfect for swimming in summer.
Practical info:
Starnberg, Tutzing, Pöcking — you can get off at different stations The lakeside walking path is completely flat — ideal for strollers Lots of picnic areas Boat tours available (separate fee) How many hours with kids? Half day is enough, full day also works
4. Salzburg (Austria) # Duration: ~1.5 hours (Regional Express) Cost: Deutschlandticket to the border, separate ticket from Freilassing (~5–10 EUR)
Technically Austria but no city is this close to Munich anywhere else. Mozart’s birthplace, Hohensalzburg Fortress, Getreidegasse.
Important: Deutschlandticket ends at the German border. Freilassing → Salzburg requires a separate ticket — very cheap.
For strollers: Old town is partly cobblestone but manageable. Castle access has an elevator.
5. Chiemsee (Chiem Lake) # Duration: ~1 hour (direct) Cost: Free with Deutschlandticket
Bavaria’s largest lake. On an island is Ludwig II’s unfinished palace Herrenchiemsee.
Practical info:
Get off at Prien am Chiemsee station Boat to the island from there (separate fee ~10 EUR) Palace museum additionally priced Beautiful lakeside walks 6. Augsburg # Duration: ~35 minutes (direct) Cost: Free with Deutschlandticket
Bavaria’s second largest city after Munich. Roman-era historic center, Fuggerei (world’s oldest social housing project), beautiful cathedrals.
With kids: Entirely within walking distance, flat streets — ideal for strollers.
7. Berchtesgaden # Duration: ~2.5 hours (with connection) Cost: Free with Deutschlandticket
A natural wonder surrounded by the Alps. Königssee (King’s Lake) can be toured by electric boat. Hitler’s Eagle’s Nest is also here — historical tours available.
For strollers: Königssee boat pier is flat. Eagle’s Nest access by bus then walk/elevator.
Useful Tools for Planning Routes # DB Navigator app — Deutsche Bahn’s official app. Shows Deutschlandticket-compatible routes. Essential.
Bayern Fahrplan — Bavaria-specific train and bus timetables.
Google Maps — Good for planning routes with public transport option.
What We Learned Traveling with a Stroller # We’ve been in Munich for over a year, exploring Bavaria with the Deutschlandticket and a double stroller. What we learned:
Station selection: Large stations generally have elevators. Small village stations often only have stairs. You can see elevator status in DB Navigator.
Train selection: Regional Express trains are spacious. S-Bahn can be crowded during peak hours. After 9 AM and after 3 PM is more comfortable.
Mountain routes: Hiking trails generally aren’t suitable for strollers — but lakesides, town centers and most tourist areas are flat.
Time planning: Everything takes longer with two small children. Leave early for day trips, bring packed lunch, plan the return around children’s sleep time.
How to Get the Deutschlandticket? # DB App (Deutsche Bahn) MVV App (Munich public transport) Any S-Bahn or U-Bahn machine Through Postbank or some banks Monthly cancellable subscription — no long-term commitment.
Frequently Asked Questions # Can I take ICE trains with the Deutschlandticket? No. Only local and regional trains — S-Bahn, U-Bahn, RB, RE, IRE. ICE and IC need a separate ticket.
How many people can use one ticket? Each ticket is personal. People traveling with you need their own tickets. Children under 6 are free.
Any difference at weekends? No, valid 24/7.
Can I bring my bike? Varies by time. Bikes are prohibited during peak weekday hours. Check DB Navigator.
Austria and Switzerland? Valid to the border. Additional ticket needed for cross-border travel — but very cheap.
summary: For 49 EUR per month you can take all local and regional trains across Germany. From Munich to Füssen, Garmisch, Salzburg, Starnberg Lake — routes we tested with two kids aged 1 and 5 and a double stroller.
title: Deutschlandticket Day Trips from Munich | Bavaria with Kids (2025)

Renting in Germany: Why Is It So Hard? # Before moving to Germany, it’s natural to think “we’ll find something to rent.” But the reality is: especially in big cities like Munich, Hamburg and Berlin, finding a rental feels like a full-time job. Days of scrolling listings, dozens of applications, very few responses.
For foreigners, this process becomes even harder. If you don’t speak German, if you have no Schufa score, or if your employment contract just started — everything becomes more difficult.
In this article I share the Munich rental search process, which documents are required, which platforms actually work and what doesn’t — all based on direct experience.
Germany’s Rental Market: The Big Picture # Tenant rights in Germany are very strong. While this sounds good for tenants, it makes landlords extremely selective about tenant choice. The result: the application process is as formal as a job application.
Munich 2025 average rents:
Apartment type Average rent Studio 1,200–1,800 EUR 1 bedroom 1,500–2,200 EUR 2 bedrooms 2,000–3,000 EUR 3 bedrooms 2,500–4,000 EUR Prices increase closer to the city center and with better public transport connections. Nebenkosten (additional costs) are generally not included in rent — electricity, water and heating are separate.
What Documents Are Required? # Standard document package for a rental application in Germany:
1. Schufa Auskunft Germany’s credit reference document. Think of it as a financial background check. Those moving to Germany for the first time won’t have a Schufa score — this is a major disadvantage. Can be requested online via Schufa.de, ~30 EUR.
2. Einkommensnachweise (Income Proof) Last 3 months’ payslips. Your net salary is expected to be 3 times the rent — meaning for 1,500 EUR rent, at least 4,500 EUR net income.
3. Mietschuldenfreiheitsbescheinigung A “no rental debt” certificate from your previous landlord. If moving from Turkey, a Turkish-language letter plus translation may work. If renting for the first time, you won’t have this — another disadvantage.
4. Personalausweis / Reisepass Kopie ID or passport photocopy.
5. Arbeitsvertrag (Employment Contract) Whether it’s fixed-term or permanent matters enormously. A permanent contract (unbefristete Stelle) is much stronger.
6. Selbstauskunft A personal information form provided by the landlord. Questions about how many people will live there, pets, smoking etc.
Which Platforms Actually Work? # Main platforms:
Platform Free? Description ImmobilienScout24 Partly Largest platform, most listings here Immowelt Partly Second largest, different listings Wg-gesucht.de Partly Best for shared housing (WG) eBay Kleinanzeigen Yes Private listings, negotiation possible Facebook Groups Yes “Wohnungen München” groups ImmobilienScout24 Premium membership: ~20 EUR per month. You get instant notifications when listings go live. In a competitive market, this makes a real difference — those who apply within the first hour have much better chances.
WG: The Shared Apartment Option # For those newly arrived in Germany, WG (Wohngemeinschaft — shared apartment) is a much more accessible starting point.
WG advantages:
Schufa score usually not required Less paperwork Much more affordable (room in Munich: 600–1,000 EUR) Gives you time to get to know the city WG disadvantages:
Not suitable for families Common areas are shared Not a long-term solution Wg-gesucht.de is the best platform for this. When creating your profile, present yourself well — choosing a flatmate is also a process in Germany.
How Does the Application Process Work? # See the listing → apply immediately. Popular listings in Munich close within hours. Write a personalized cover letter — sending just documents isn’t enough. Briefly introduce yourself, your work, and why you want this apartment. Get a Besichtigungstermin (viewing appointment) — viewings are often held with multiple candidates simultaneously. Have your document package ready — being able to hand it over at the viewing is an advantage. Wait for feedback — sometimes there’s silence for weeks; this is normal. Extra Challenges for Foreigners # No Schufa: Since you have no history in Germany, your Schufa score is zero. Counter-measures:
Offer a higher deposit (6 months instead of 3) Provide a Bürge (guarantor) — must be someone resident in Germany Get a strong reference letter from your employer Language barrier: The vast majority of listings and contracts are in German. Use the DeepL translation tool, and always get important documents translated before signing.
Receiving “no” answers: Applying to dozens of places and getting no responses is very common. This is not personal — landlords often prefer local Germans with established references.
Once You Sign a Lease # Kaution (Deposit): Maximum 3 months’ rent. Must be deposited in a tenant account — the landlord cannot use this money. Returned when you move out if there’s no damage.
Mieterverein membership: Consider joining a tenants’ association — about 10 EUR per month, provides legal support in any disputes.
Übergabeprotokoll: Key handover record. Document the condition of the apartment at handover — photograph every scratch and mark. You’ll need this when moving out.
My Experience # I started searching 2 months before moving to Munich. I got an ImmobilienScout24 Premium membership and applied to 3-4 listings per day. Zero responses in the first month. In the second month I got 3 viewings, one of which came back positive.
Since I had no Schufa score, I offered extra deposit. My employer provided a reference letter. The process took 2.5 months.
Most critical lesson: Start searching before you move to Germany. Temporary accommodation (Airbnb, hotel) after arrival is extremely expensive.
Frequently Asked Questions # Can you find an apartment without Schufa? Difficult but not impossible. A higher deposit offer, strong employer reference and a personal cover letter make a difference.
Is Anmeldung required for a rental contract? No — you sign the rental contract first, then do Anmeldung. But you need the rental contract to do Anmeldung.
How much are Nebenkosten? Usually 20-30% of rent. On 1,500 EUR rent, expect 300-450 EUR Nebenkosten.
Can you find a rental with pets? Much harder. Landlord approval is required and many refuse.
Are there cheaper alternatives outside Munich? Yes — surrounding cities like Augsburg, Landsberg am Lech, Freising are 30-40% cheaper. S-Bahn connections to Munich are good.
Summary: What Should You Do? # Start searching at least 2 months before moving Get ImmobilienScout24 Premium — instant notifications are critical Prepare your document package in advance — Schufa, income proof, employment contract Write a personal cover letter — don’t use the same text for every listing Consider WG not as a backup but as a starting point Look at surrounding cities too — Munich’s S-Bahn network is extensive
summary: Finding a rental in Munich is one of Germany’s most difficult bureaucratic processes. Schufa score, employment contract, reference letters and hundreds of applications — it gets even harder for foreigners. First-hand experience.
title: Renting an Apartment in Germany | The Reality of Finding Housing in Munich (2025)

“If I Get a Bulgarian License, Will Germany Accept It?” — The Clear Answer # For Turkish people who have moved to Germany and hold Bulgarian citizenship, this question is inevitable. The logic goes like this:
“I’m a Bulgarian citizen. If I convert my Turkish license to a Bulgarian one, I’ll have an EU license. Germany will accept it.”
This logic is theoretically attractive, practically wrong. I tried this route. I spent time, I spent money, I made several trips to Sofia. The result: Germany does not recognize this license.
In this article, I’m telling you that process step by step, exactly as it happened.
Personal Background # I was born and raised in Istanbul. Since my father’s side are Bulgarian immigrants, I had a long-running citizenship process. In August 2024, I obtained Bulgarian citizenship and became a dual citizen. In February 2025, I moved to Germany.
After settling in Germany, the first alternative that came to mind: “I’ll convert my Turkish license to a Bulgarian one, problem solved.”
There’s serious information chaos online about this topic. Some say “it works,” others say “I sorted it through contacts,” and others say “nobody asked me anything” without giving any details. The reason I’m writing this article is precisely that confusion.
What’s Required to Transfer a License in Bulgaria? # To do this in Bulgaria, you first need an active residence permit and registered address. Without an address registration, you can’t even start the process.
Required documents:
Active address registration and residence in Bulgaria Original Turkish license + photocopy Passport photos Medical certificate suitable for license renewal Knowledge of Bulgarian or interpreter support License fee (credit card accepted) Medical Certificate: Simpler Than Expected # You don’t necessarily need to go to Sofia for the medical certificate. I did this at a small medical facility near Kardzhali. The doctor asked a few basic questions — there was no real examination. I paid about 30 leva and received the certificate the same day.
Sofia KAT Centers: Where Reality Begins # KAT (Kontrol na Avtomobilniya Transport) — the institution that handles traffic and license matters in Bulgaria. There are multiple centers in Sofia.
When I entered the first center, everything was in Bulgarian: signs, forms, instructions. The number of English-speaking staff was extremely low.
What Happened on the First Attempt? # At the first center I was told my address wasn’t registered there and was directed to another center. At the second center I took a number, waited, and stood before the officer. I tried to speak English — the officer didn’t know English and didn’t want to process the application. It was clearly stated: “Come back with someone who speaks Bulgarian.”
What Happened on the Second Attempt? # On the next attempt, the photocopy and passport photos were missing. I went out, got them, came back, took another number. This time a different officer came, and again the process stopped due to the language barrier.
At this point you understand one thing clearly:
The system is officially theoretical, but in practice entirely dependent on the human factor.
Without an Interpreter, It Simply Doesn’t Work # In a nearby mall, through a shop assistant who had gone through this process, I got the number of an interpreter who spoke Turkish and Bulgarian. With the interpreter, we returned to the center. This time everything moved fast. Forms were filled, questions asked and answered.
The plain truth: If you don’t speak Bulgarian, this process almost doesn’t work without an interpreter.
After Application: 30-Day Wait # During the application, you’re given a document. This is needed when collecting the license — don’t lose it.
The waiting period is approximately 30 days. During this time you keep your Turkish license and can legally continue driving.
I Collected the License — And Everything Changed # Around day 25, I received notification that the license was ready. I made a day trip to Sofia and completed the collection with interpreter support.
During collection, my Turkish license was taken and the Bulgarian license was given in its place. Everything seemed fine.
Until I looked at the back of the license.
The TR Code: The Summary of the Entire Process # On the back of the license was a code in the format TR-XXXXX.
This code indicates that the country of original issue was Turkey. The document had changed, but the legal origin had not.
The Clear Answer From Germany # I went directly to the driving license office (Führerscheinstelle) in Germany and asked:
“Can I use this Bulgarian license in Germany?”
The answer was clear and definitive: No.
Even though the license was issued by Bulgaria, because its origin was non-EU, Germany still treated it as a Turkish license.
Why Doesn’t It Work? The Legal Explanation # EU Driving License Directive 2006/126/EC clearly states: A member state must recognize a license issued by another member state — but only if that license was issued according to that member state’s own criteria.
When Bulgaria converts a Turkish license, it records the origin of the document. Germany sees this record and evaluates the license not as EU-based, but as Turkish-based.
Conclusion: This route looks attractive in theory, but legally it doesn’t work.
What Was the Cost? # Item Estimate Sofia round trips (2 trips) 150–300 EUR Medical certificate ~15 EUR Interpreter fee ~50–100 EUR License processing fee ~50 EUR Lost time 2–3 working days Total ~265–465 EUR + time And the result: a Bulgarian license that can’t be used in Germany.
So What Should You Do? # The only valid way to get a driving license in Germany is to go through the fahrschule process. This process is long and expensive, but it produces a legally valid result.
For the detailed process: Driving License in Germany | Complete Guide
Frequently Asked Questions # Can’t I transfer my Turkish license if I’m a Bulgarian citizen? You can transfer it — but the resulting Bulgarian license is evaluated in Germany the same as a Turkish license. Because the EU directive records the country of origin.
Are there people who’ve successfully used this route? Online there are people saying “I sorted it” but I couldn’t find evidence of any of them getting official confirmation from a Führerscheinstelle. The official answer is clear: no.
Doesn’t dual citizenship provide an advantage here? For citizenship matters, yes. For driving license matters, no. The license directive looks at the country where the license was first issued, not citizenship.
What if I get a fresh license from scratch in Bulgaria? In theory, yes — but this requires genuinely residing in Bulgaria, passing the theory test in Bulgarian, and completing practical training there. Very difficult in practice.
Is there a lawyer who knows about this? Specialized lawyers exist, but those I consulted reached the same conclusion: the EU directive is clear, there’s no legal basis for appeal.
Conclusion: There Are No Shortcuts # Looking back today: time was spent, money was spent, but the legal result was zero.
If someone reading this is making a similar plan — the greatest gain is knowing this truth from the very beginning.
The only valid way to get a driving license in Germany is the fahrschule process. Long and expensive, but the only way that works.
summary: I obtained Bulgarian citizenship and converted my Turkish license to a Bulgarian one. Sofia KAT centers, mandatory interpreter, 30-day wait and then the clear answer from Germany: no, it doesn’t work. The EU directive doesn’t allow it.
title: Can You Drive in Germany with a Bulgarian License? | Turkish→Bulgarian Transfer Reality (2025)

content:
Almanya’ya Taşındıktan Sonra Fark Edilen İlk Gerçekler # Almanya’ya taşındığınızda, birçok şey aynı kalıyor gibi görünür. Eviniz, işiniz, günlük rutininiz yavaş yavaş oturur. Ancak bazı konular vardır ki farkına geç varırsınız. Ehliyet bunlardan biridir.
Taşındıktan sonraki ilk aylarda kimse size “altı ay sonra araba kullanamazsın” diye hatırlatma yapmaz. Bu bilgi sessizce durur ve siz fark etmezseniz süre kendiliğinden dolar.
Bu yazı, Almanya’da ehliyet yenileme süreci, Bulgaristan üzerinden ehliyet çevirme ve bu yolun neden işe yaramadığına dair birebir yaşanmış bir deneyimi aktarıyor.
Kişisel Arka Plan: Türkiye’den Bulgaristan’a, Oradan Almanya’ya # Ben İstanbul’da doğup büyüdüm. Baba tarafından Bulgaristan göçmeni olduğumuz için yıllardır devam eden bir vatandaşlık sürecim vardı. Ağustos 2024’te Bulgaristan vatandaşlığını aldım ve çifte vatandaş oldum. Şubat 2025’te Almanya’ya taşındım.
Almanya’ya yerleştiğimde aklımdaki soru basitti:
Türkiye’den alınmış bir ehliyet Almanya’da ne kadar süre geçerli?
Almanya’da Ehliyetle İlgili Hukuki Çerçeve # Almanya’da adres kaydınızı (Anmeldung) yaptırdığınız andan itibaren süre işlemeye başlar. Türkiye’den alınmış bir ehliyet, Avrupa Birliği dışı kabul edildiği için yalnızca 6 ay geçerlidir.
Bu sürenin sonunda “idare eder”, “kontrol edilmez” gibi ihtimallerin hukuki karşılığı yoktur. Almanya bu konuda gri alan bırakmaz. Bu nedenle süreci erkenden anlamaya çalıştım.
“Bulgaristan Üzerinden Çözüm” Fikrinin Ortaya Çıkışı # Bulgaristan vatandaşı olmam, doğal olarak başka bir kapı açtı. “Türk ehliyetini Bulgar ehliyetine çevirirsem, sonuçta elimde AB ehliyeti olur” düşüncesi yalnızca benim değil, bu durumda olan birçok kişinin ilk refleksi.
İnternette bu konuda ciddi bir bilgi karmaşası var. Kimisi “olur” diyor, kimisi “tanıdıkla hallettim” diyor, kimisi ise hiçbir detay vermeden “bana kimse bir şey sormadı” diye anlatıyor. Bu yazıyı yazma sebebim de tam olarak bu karmaşa.
Bulgaristan Üzerinden İşlem Yapabilmek İçin Gerekli Şartlar # Benim Bulgaristan’da aktif bir oturumum ve Sofya’da kayıtlı bir adresim vardı. Bu önemli, çünkü adres kaydınız yoksa Bulgaristan’da bu işlemleri başlatmanız zaten mümkün değil.
Gerekli belgeler:
Bulgaristan’da aktif adres kaydı ve oturum Türk ehliyetinin aslı + fotokopisi Vesikalık fotoğraflar Ehliyet yenilemeye uygun sağlık raporu Yerel dil bilgisi veya tercüman desteği Ehliyet ücreti (kredi kartı ile ödeme yapılabiliyor) Sağlık Raporu Süreci: Beklenenden Daha Basit # Sağlık raporu için Sofya’ya gitmek şart değil. Ben Kırcaali tarafında küçük bir sağlık kuruluşunda bu işlemi yaptım. Doktor birkaç temel soru sordu, gerçek anlamda bir muayene yapılmadı. Yaklaşık 30 leva ödedim ve raporu aynı gün aldım.
Sofya’daki KAT Merkezlerinde Gerçeklerle Yüzleşme # Asıl gerçek, Sofya’daki KAT (Kontrol na Avtomobilniya Transport) merkezlerine girdiğinizde başlıyor. Tabelalar, formlar ve yönlendirmeler tamamen Bulgarca.
İlk gittiğim KAT merkezinde adresimin oraya bağlı olmadığı söylendi ve başka bir merkeze yönlendirildim. İkinci merkezde sıra aldım, numaram yandı, memurun karşısına geçtim. İngilizce konuşmaya çalıştım ancak memur İngilizce bilmiyordu ve işlem yapmak istemedi. Açıkça “Bulgarca konuşan biriyle gel” denildi.
Evrak, Dil ve İnsan Faktörü # Bir sonraki denemede ehliyet fotokopisi ve vesikalık fotoğraf eksikti. Dışarı çıkıp fotokopi çektirdim, geri döndüm, tekrar sıra aldım. Bu sefer başka bir memur geldi ve yine dil nedeniyle süreç durdu.
Bu noktada şunu net anlıyorsunuz:
Sistem teoride resmî, pratikte ise tamamen insan faktörüne bağlı.
Tercümanla Tanışma: Sistemin Yazılmayan Kuralı # Yakınlardaki bir AVM’de, daha önce bu süreci yaşamış bir mağaza çalışanı sayesinde Türkçe–Bulgarca bilen bir tercümanın numarasına ulaştım. Tercümanla birlikte tekrar merkeze döndük. Bu sefer her şey hızlandı. Formlar dolduruldu, sorular soruldu, cevaplar verildi.
Açık gerçek şu: Bulgarca bilmiyorsanız, bu süreç tercümansız neredeyse yürümüyor.
Başvuru Sonrası Bekleme Süreci # Başvuru sırasında size bir belge veriliyor. Bu belgeyi ehliyeti teslim alırken göstermeniz gerekiyor — kaybetmeyin.
Başvurudan sonra yaklaşık 30 gün bekleniyor. Bu süre boyunca Türk ehliyeti sizde kalıyor ve yasal olarak araç kullanmaya devam edebiliyorsunuz.
Ehliyet Teslimi ve Kritik Detay # Yaklaşık 25. günde ehliyetin hazır olduğu bilgisi geldi. Günübirlik Sofya’ya gittim ve yine tercüman desteğiyle teslim sürecini tamamladım.
Teslim sırasında Türk ehliyetim alındı ve yerine Bulgar ehliyeti verildi. Her şey tamam gibi görünüyordu. Ta ki ehliyetin arkasına bakana kadar.
TR Kodu: Tüm Sürecin Özeti # Ehliyetin arkasında TR-XXXXX şeklinde bir kod yer alıyordu. Bu kod, ehliyetin ilk alındığı ülkenin Türkiye olduğunu gösteriyor.
Yani belge değişmişti ama hukuki köken değişmemişti.
Almanya’da Alınan Net Cevap # Almanya’daki ehliyet dairesine (Führerscheinstelle) gidip doğrudan sordum:
“Bu ehliyeti Almanya’da kullanabilir miyim?”
Cevap netti: Hayır.
Ehliyet Bulgaristan tarafından verilmiş olsa bile, kökeni AB dışı olduğu için Almanya açısından hâlâ Türk ehliyeti muamelesi görüyordu.
Almanya’daki Zorunlu Süreçle Tanışma # Bundan sonra süreç herkes için benzer ilerliyor: ehliyet kursu, teorik sınav, pratik dersler ve eğitmenin onayı. Türkiye’de yıllarca araç kullanmış olmanız burada otomatik bir avantaj sağlamıyor.
Gerçekçi beklentiler:
⏱ Süre 5–6 ay 💶 Maliyet 2.000 € ve üzeri 📝 İlk sınavda başarı Birçok kişi başarısız oluyor Net Sonuç: Kestirme Yol Yok # Bugün geriye dönüp baktığımda şunu net söyleyebilirim: Bulgaristan üzerinden ehliyet almak, Almanya’da bir kestirme yol değildir.
Eğer bu yazıyı okuyan biri benzer bir plan yapıyorsa, en büyük kazanım bu gerçeği en baştan bilmek olur.
summary: Almanya’ya taşındıktan sonra ehliyet yenileme sürecini erken öğrenmezseniz bedeli ağır olabilir. Bulgaristan üzerinden çözüm arayışı ve neden işe yaramadığı — birebir deneyim.
title: Almanya'da Ehliyet Yenileme Süreci | Türk & Bulgar Ehliyeti Gerçeği

The First Reality After Moving to Germany # When you move to Germany, many things seem to remain the same. Your home, job, and daily routine slowly fall into place. However, there are topics you only realize too late. The driving license is one of them.
In the first few months after moving, no one will remind you that “you won’t be able to drive a car in six months.” This information sits quietly in the background, and if you don’t notice it, the time simply runs out.
This post shares a first-hand experience of the driving license exchange process in Germany, the attempt via Bulgaria, and why this path didn’t work.
Personal Background: From Turkey to Bulgaria, then to Germany # I was born and raised in Istanbul. Since we are Bulgarian immigrants on my father’s side, I had a long-standing citizenship process. In August 2024, I obtained Bulgarian citizenship and became a dual citizen. In February 2025, I moved to Germany.
When I settled in Germany, my question was simple: How long is a driving license issued in Turkey valid in Germany?
The Legal Framework in Germany # The clock starts ticking the moment you register your address (Anmeldung). Since a license issued in Turkey is considered non-EU, it is only valid for 6 months.
After this period, there is no legal room for “it’ll be fine” or “they won’t check.” Germany leaves no gray areas here. That’s why I tried to understand the process early on.
Which Countries’ Licenses are Directly Valid in Germany? # Here is the general overview:
License Country Validity in Germany EU member states Valid indefinitely, no exchange required Switzerland, Norway, Iceland Directly valid Turkey 6 months from Anmeldung Ukraine, Serbia etc. Depends on the country, check required USA, Canada Agreement depends on the state/province, usually 6 months The Idea of the “Bulgarian Solution” # Being a Bulgarian citizen naturally opened another door. The thought: “If I exchange my Turkish license for a Bulgarian one, I’ll eventually have an EU license.” This is often the first reflex of many people in this situation.
There is significant information chaos online regarding this. Some say “it works,” others say “I handled it through contacts,” and others provide no details. This confusion is exactly why I am writing this post.
Requirements for the Process in Bulgaria # I had an active residence permit in Bulgaria and a registered address in Sofia. This is important because without a registered address, you cannot even start this process in Bulgaria.
Required documents:
Active address registration and residence in Bulgaria Original Turkish license + copy Passport photos Medical certificate suitable for license renewal Local language knowledge or interpreter support License fee (payment by credit card is possible) The Medical Certificate Process: Simpler than Expected # You don’t necessarily have to go to Sofia for the medical certificate. I did this at a small medical facility near Kardzhali. The doctor asked a few basic questions; there was no real physical exam. I paid about 30 leva and received the certificate on the same day.
Facing Reality at the KAT Centers in Sofia # The real truth begins when you enter the KAT (Kontrol na Avtomobilniya Transport) centers in Sofia. Signs, forms, and instructions are entirely in Bulgarian.
At the first KAT center, I was told my address wasn’t registered there and was directed to another center. At the second center, I took a number, waited, and finally stood before the officer. I tried to speak English, but the officer didn’t know English and didn’t want to process the application. I was told clearly: “Come back with someone who speaks Bulgarian.”
Documents, Language, and the Human Factor # On the next attempt, the copy of the license and passport photos were missing. I went out, got copies made, came back, and waited again. This time another officer came, and again the process stopped due to the language barrier.
At this point, you understand one thing clearly:
The system is theoretically official, but in practice, it is entirely dependent on the human factor.
Meeting the Interpreter: The Unwritten Rule of the System # In a nearby mall, thanks to a shop assistant who had gone through this process, I got the number of an interpreter who spoke Turkish and Bulgarian. With the interpreter, we returned to the center. This time everything moved fast. Forms were filled, questions were asked and answered.
The plain truth is: If you don’t speak Bulgarian, this process almost doesn’t work without an interpreter.
The Waiting Period After Application # During the application, you are given a document. You must show this document when collecting the license — do not lose it.
After the application, you wait for about 30 days. During this time, you keep your Turkish license and can legally continue to drive.
Delivery of the License and a Critical Detail # Around the 25th day, I was notified that the license was ready. I went to Sofia for the day and completed the delivery process with interpreter support.
During delivery, my Turkish license was taken, and the Bulgarian license was given in its place. Everything seemed fine. Until I looked at the back of the license.
The TR Code: The Summary of the Whole Process # On the back of the license, there was a code in the format TR-XXXXX. This code indicates that the country of original issue was Turkey.
This meant: The document had changed, but the legal origin had not.
The Clear Answer from Germany # I went directly to the driving license office (Führerscheinstelle) in Germany and asked: “Can I use this license in Germany?”
The answer was clear: No.
Even though the license was issued by Bulgaria, it was still treated as a Turkish license by Germany because its origin was non-EU.
Why the Bulgarian Path Doesn’t Work: Legal Explanation # When looking at the situation from the perspective of EU directives, it becomes clear. According to EU Driving License Directive 2006/126/EC, a member state must recognize a license issued by another member state — but only if that license was issued according to that member state’s own criteria.
When Bulgaria exchanges a Turkish license, the origin of the document is recorded. Germany sees this record and evaluates the license not as EU-based, but as Turkish-based.
So, this path looks attractive in theory, but it doesn’t work legally.
Facing the Mandatory Process in Germany # From here, the process is similar for everyone: driving school, theory test, practical lessons, and instructor approval. The fact that you have driven for years in Turkey doesn’t give you an automatic advantage here.
What You Need to Know About the Theory Test # The exam can be taken in German, English, or Turkish. Total of 30 questions, a maximum of 10 error points allowed. Official apps for studying: DEGENER or Führerschein 2024. Average study time: 4–8 weeks. About Practical Lessons # Driver training in Germany is very different from Turkey. The instructor will not send you to the exam until they see you as competent. How many lessons you take depends entirely on the instructor — this directly affects the cost.
Duration, Cost, and Realistic Expectations # ⏱ Total duration 5–6 months 💶 Average cost €2,000 – €3,500 �� Theory exam fee ~€25 🚗 Practical exam fee ~€200 📚 Average per lesson ~€50–70 ❌ Failing the first time Very common Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) # Can I rent a car in Germany with a Turkish license? Within the first 6 months after Anmeldung, yes. Some rental companies may request additional documents.
What happens if I exceed 6 months? Driving becomes legally prohibited. In the event of an accident, insurance may be considered invalid.
Is Anmeldung required to start a driving school? No, Anmeldung is not mandatory to start. But it is required when taking the exam.
Which city is cheaper to get a license? Small cities are usually cheaper. Munich and Hamburg are among the most expensive.
Can the theory exam be taken in Turkish? Yes, the theory exam can be selected in Turkish.
Final Result: There Are No Shortcuts # Looking back today, I can say one thing clearly: Obtaining a license via Bulgaria is not a shortcut in Germany.
Time was spent, money was spent, but the legal result was zero. If someone reading this is making a similar plan, the greatest gain would be to know this truth from the start.
The process in Germany is long and expensive, but it is the only valid way.
Summary: What Should You Do? # Note your Anmeldung date — the 6-month period starts there. Apply to a driving school immediately — good schools have waiting lists. Start theory study early — download the app, 20 minutes every day. Don’t research the Bulgaria path — that’s what this post is for. Set aside a budget — it rarely falls below €2,500.
summary: Failing to plan the driving license exchange process early after moving to Germany can be costly. My experience trying to exchange a license via Bulgaria — and why it failed.
title: Driving License Exchange in Germany | The Reality of Turkish & Bulgarian Licenses
